Professor View 01 / 27
Travel English -- 48 Aulas

Izabel Cristina de Pieri

Business English Executivo para Reuniões Internacionais e Liderança

B2 (Intermediário Superior) Valinhos, SP CEO — AVRT (Componentes Eletrônicos) 90 min · 2x/semana · Online
Progresso Geral0%
Velocidade:
Nome

Izabel Cristina de Pieri

Idade

40 anos

Cidade

Valinhos, SP

Profissão

CEO da AVRT — startup americana de componentes eletrônicos e tecnologia wireless

Nível

B2 (Intermediário Superior) — vocabulário técnico e compreensão altos, produção oral espontânea trava

Foco

Business English executivo: reuniões semanais com equipe americana, reportar dados financeiros, phrasal verbs e fluência espontânea

Frequência

2x por semana — Online (Zoom)

Total de Aulas

96 aulas (90 min) — 100% Personalizado

Personagem-Jornada

De: Uma CEO que entende inglês, lê relatórios e domina o vocabulário técnico (cash flow, taxation, ERP, account payable), mas TRAVA ao montar frases em tempo real — esquece a estrutura, troca para o português e dá respostas curtas nas reuniões de quinta com a equipe americana. Sabe exatamente onde está o gap: "I have a lot of vocabulary, but when I make a phrase I don't remember."

Para: Uma líder que abre, reporta, questiona, argumenta e encerra a reunião de quinta em inglês — com a mesma autoridade que tem em português. Fala de números e estratégia sem hesitar, usa phrasal verbs e idioms como um executivo americano, e faz perguntas complexas sem o "translation freeze". Em 96 aulas.

Promessa Transformadora

De "travar ao montar a frase" a conduzir a Thursday Meeting com fluência executiva. O problema da Izabel não é vocabulário nem compreensão: é produção oral espontânea e estrutura em tempo real. Cada aula prioriza a fala em cenários reais dela — a reunião semanal, o report financeiro, a argumentação de decisões — num ambiente seguro para errar e construir confiança de forma incremental. A reunião de quinta é a estrela-guia de todo o programa.

Forças
  • Vocabulário técnico financeiro e tributário sólido (cash flow, invoice, taxation, ERP)
  • Fluência comunicativa — transmite ideias complexas mesmo com erros de estrutura
  • Alta inteligência contextual e raciocínio estratégico claro
  • Auto-consciência pedagógica: sabe exatamente qual é o gap
  • Experiência de imersão real (Austrália/Perth) e trabalho em empresa americana (AOL)
Pontos de Melhoria
  • Produção oral espontânea trava na montagem da frase em tempo real
  • Erros recorrentes de estrutura: concordância (they doesn't), preposições, tenses
  • Ausência de phrasal verbs e idioms americanos no repertório ativo
  • Formação de perguntas: dificuldade em construir sem sujeito ou inversão
  • Pronúncia: transferência do português (stress silábico, -ed do passado)

Metodologia

1. Desbloqueio da Produção Oral

A Izabel sabe mais do que produz. Cada aula prioriza a fala espontânea (role-plays, mini-apresentações, produção livre) para destravar a montagem de frases em tempo real.

2. A Thursday Meeting como Estrela-Guia

Todo conteúdo gira em torno do universo real dela: a reunião semanal com Andy e os sócios americanos, o report financeiro, o projeto ERP e a reforma tributária.

3. Phrasal Verbs & Idioms Executivos

Introdução sistemática dos phrasal verbs e expressões idiomáticas que executivos americanos usam de verdade — o gap que mais a entusiasmou no diagnóstico.

4. Estrutura sob Pressão

Drills de formação de perguntas, concordância e tenses em ritmo de reunião, para que a estrutura correta saia automática — sem o "translation freeze".

5. Erro sem Vergonha

Feedback adiado durante a fala livre e ritmo respeitoso. A Izabel se frustra com métodos engessados; aqui o erro vira aprendizado, nunca constrangimento.

Mapa de Personalidade

IntrovertidoExtrovertido
Aprendiz leitura/escritaAprendiz auditivo
ImpulsivoReflexivo
Tolerância ao erro baixaTolerância ao erro alta
Energia caóticaEnergia calma e estruturada
Inseguro em inglêsConfiante em inglês
Foco acadêmicoFoco profissional
Aula 01 -- Pre-class

Mapping Your Executive Voice

Introducing yourself and your role as CEO of AVRT. Key words: be in charge of, report to, deal with, take care of, come up with, background, current role, responsibilities, cash flow, in-house, challenge, on a daily basis. Structures: present simple for your role + forming questions.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Be in charge of -- to be responsible for an area, team, or decision
"I am in charge of finance and operations at AVRT."
Report to -- to have someone as your direct manager
"I report to Andy and the American partners."
Deal with -- to take action on a problem or task (phrasal verb -- lidar com)
"I deal with taxes, cash flow, and suppliers every day."
Take care of -- to be responsible for handling something (phrasal verb -- cuidar de)
"My team takes care of the day-to-day operations."
Come up with -- to think of an idea or solution (phrasal verb -- criar, bolar)
"We came up with a plan to wait for the tax reform."
Background -- your past experience, education, and training
"My background is in finance and taxation."
Current role -- the position you hold now
"In my current role, I lead the whole operation."
Responsibilities -- the duties and tasks your job requires
"My responsibilities cover finance, tax, and stock control."
Cash flow -- the money coming in and going out of a business
"I monitor the cash flow on a weekly basis."
In-house -- done inside the company, not outsourced
"We keep the accounting in-house instead of hiring a firm."
Challenge -- a demanding problem or task you must handle (desafio)
"My main challenge right now is the new tax reform."
On a daily basis -- every day, as part of your routine (diariamente)
"On a daily basis, I review invoices and approve payments."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Be in charge of
Report to
Deal with
Take care of
Come up with
Background
Current role
Responsibilities
Cash flow
In-house
Challenge
On a daily basis

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Izabel is the CEO of AVRT. She is in charge of finance and operations, and she reports to Andy and the board. On a daily basis, she deals with cash flow, taxes, and stock control. Her team takes care of the invoices and payments, but the big decisions come to her. "My background is in finance," she says. Her main challenge right now is the new tax reform. The partners don't have all the numbers yet, so they ask her a lot of questions. When a new partner joins the call, Izabel says: "In my current role, I keep most of the accounting in-house." Then she asks back: "What do you do, and who do you report to?"

1. Why do we write "she deals with" and "she reports" with an -s?
A Because the action happened in the past.
B Because in the present simple, he/she/it adds -s to the verb.
C Because "Izabel" is plural.
2. "The partners don't have all the numbers yet." Why "don't" and not "doesn't"?
A Because "the partners" is plural (they), so we use "don't".
B Because "don't" is more polite than "doesn't".
C Because the sentence is a question.
3. Which question does Izabel form correctly?
A "What you do, and who you report to?"
B "What do you do, and who do you report to?"
C "What do you do, and who report you to?"

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple & Questions

GRAMMAR

How to describe your role and ask questions (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
I / You / We / They + verbFatos e rotinas (sem -s)I deal with taxes.
He / She / It + verb+s3ª pessoa SEMPRE leva -sShe reports to Andy.
don't / doesn'tNegativa: they/we/you don't; he/she/it doesn'tThey don't have the data.
WH + do/does + subject + verbPerguntar sobre alguém. NUNCA esqueça o sujeitoWhat do you do? Who does she report to?
am / is / areIdentidade e cargo: I am the CEO. My challenge is the tax reform.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"I am finance and operations."
"I Andy and the board."
"On a daily basis, I cash flow and taxes."
"My team the operations."
"What you do at AVRT?"
"My main right now is the tax reform."

Stage 2: Put the Introduction in Order

Order

Put the steps of an executive self-introduction in the correct order.

?Explain what you deal with on a daily basis.
?Ask the other person a question about their role.
?Say your name and your company.
?Mention your main challenge right now.
?State your role and who you report to.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Hi everyone, I am Izabel, the CEO of AVRT.
Oi pessoal, sou a Izabel, CEO da AVRT.
I am in charge of finance and operations.
Sou responsável por finanças e operações.
On a daily basis, I deal with cash flow and taxes.
Diariamente, eu lido com fluxo de caixa e impostos.
I report to Andy and the board.
Eu me reporto ao Andy e ao conselho.
What do you do, and who do you report to?
O que você faz, e a quem você se reporta?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

A new partner asks you to describe your role. What do you say?
A "I do finance things and other things."
B "I am the CEO. I am in charge of finance and operations."
C "I am CEO, I make the finance."
Someone asks: "Who do you report to?" You respond:
A "I report for Andy."
B "I report to Andy and the board."
C "I am report to the board."
You want to ask a new partner about their job. The correct question is:
A "What you do for the group?"
B "What would like to know your role?"
C "What do you do, and who do you report to?"
You want to describe your daily work. You say:
A "On a daily basis, I deal with cash flow and my team takes care of the invoices."
B "Every days I deal the cash flow and my team take care the invoices."
C "I daily deal cash flow, team take care invoices."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

A new American partner joins the Thursday call and asks: "So, what exactly do you do at AVRT?" Introduce yourself: your role, who you report to, your daily responsibilities, and your main challenge. Use the present simple. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 1

1Hi everyone, I am Izabel, the CEO of AVRT.Oi pessoal, sou a Izabel, CEO da AVRT.
2I am in charge of finance and operations.Sou responsável por finanças e operações.
3I report to Andy and the board.Eu me reporto ao Andy e ao conselho.
4On a daily basis, I deal with cash flow and taxes.Diariamente, eu lido com fluxo de caixa e impostos.
5My main challenge right now is the tax reform.Meu principal desafio agora é a reforma tributária.
Aula 02 -- Pre-class

The CEO Introducing Herself on a Thursday Call

Opening and running the weekly meeting. Key words: oversee, liaise with, ensure, track, handle, run operations, lead a team, workflow, streamline, milestone, supplier, deadline. Structures: present simple + frequency adverbs (always, usually, often, rarely).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Oversee -- to watch over work and make sure it is done well (supervisionar)
"I oversee the whole business at AVRT."
Liaise with -- to stay in contact and work closely with someone (articular-se com)
"I liaise with our suppliers every week."
Ensure -- to make certain that something happens
"I ensure that every figure is correct before the board sees it."
Track -- to follow the progress or status of something (acompanhar)
"I track the key figures during the week."
Handle -- to deal with a task, a client, or a problem
"My team handles the invoices and daily tasks."
Run operations -- to be in charge of running the daily business
"I run operations across the whole company."
Lead a team -- to guide and take responsibility for a group of people
"I lead a small team here in Valinhos."
Workflow -- the sequence of steps to complete work (fluxo de trabalho)
"Our approval workflow is clear and fast."
Streamline -- to make a process simpler and more efficient
"We streamlined the order process this year."
Milestone -- an important point or goal in a project (marco)
"Closing the tax review was a big milestone."
Supplier -- a company that provides goods to your business (fornecedor)
"Our main supplier ships the components in two weeks."
Deadline -- the time by which something must be finished (prazo)
"The reporting deadline is Wednesday evening."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Oversee
Liaise with
Ensure
Track
Handle
Run operations
Lead a team
Workflow
Streamline
Milestone
Supplier
Deadline

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Every Thursday, Izabel runs the weekly call. She oversees the whole business and leads a small team. She usually opens with the cash flow, and then she moves to operations. During the week she always tracks the key figures and liaises with the suppliers, so she is always ready before the meeting. Her team handles the invoices, and she often streamlines a process when she sees a problem. The partners frequently ask difficult questions, and she does not always have every answer. When that happens, she rarely freezes. She simply says, "Let me check that and get back to you."

1. Why do we write "she runs", "she oversees", and "she tracks" with an -s?
A Because the actions are finished in the past.
B Because in the present simple, he/she/it adds -s to the verb.
C Because "Izabel" is plural.
2. Look at "she usually opens" and "she is always ready". Where does the frequency adverb go?
A Before the main verb, but after the verb "be".
B Always at the very end of the sentence.
C Always at the very beginning of the sentence.
3. What does Izabel say when she does not know an answer?
A She stops talking and switches to Portuguese.
B "Let me check that and get back to you."
C "I don't know, next question please."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Simple & Frequency Adverbs

GRAMMAR

Routines and how often you do them (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
He / She / It + verb+sRotina na 3ª pessoa SEMPRE leva -sShe runs the call.
Advérbio + verbo principalalways, usually, often, rarely vêm ANTES do verboI usually open with the cash flow.
be + advérbiocom o verbo "be", o advérbio vem DEPOISShe is always ready.
don't / doesn'tNegativa: they/we/you don't; he/she/it doesn'tI don't always have the answer.
How often + do/doesPara perguntar a frequência: How often do you run the call?

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct word. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Every Thursday, I the weekly call."
"I open with the cash flow."
"She the whole business."
"I that every figure is correct."
"She is ready before the call."
"How often you liaise with your suppliers?"

Stage 2: Put the Opening in Order

Order

Put the steps of opening the Thursday call in the correct order.

?Give a one-line update on the cash flow.
?Invite the partners to ask questions.
?Greet everyone and welcome the new partners.
?Move to the operations update.
?Say what you will cover today.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Good morning, everyone. Let me give you a quick update.
Bom dia a todos. Deixem-me dar uma atualização rápida.
I usually open with the cash flow, then move to operations.
Eu geralmente começo pelo fluxo de caixa e depois vou para operações.
I run operations and I lead a small team here.
Eu comando as operações e lidero uma pequena equipe aqui.
That's a good question. Let me check that and get back to you.
Boa pergunta. Deixe-me verificar e te retorno.
How often do you liaise with your suppliers?
Com que frequência você se articula com seus fornecedores?

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You are opening the Thursday call. What do you say first?
A "Okay, we begin, I talk about money now."
B "Good morning, everyone. Let me give you a quick update."
C "Hello, I am start the meeting for the numbers."
Which sentence puts the frequency adverb in the right place?
A "I open always the call with the cash flow."
B "I always open the call with the cash flow."
C "I open the call always with the cash flow."
A partner asks a question you cannot answer right now. You say:
A "That's a good question. Let me check that and get back to you."
B "I don't know." (and stop talking)
C "This is not my responsibility."
You want to describe your role. Which sentence is correct?
A "I run operations, oversee the reporting, and lead a small team."
B "I running operation, oversee the report, lead team small."
C "I am run operations and I am lead the team."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Open your Thursday call for a group of new partners. Greet them, say what you oversee and run, describe your typical week using frequency adverbs (usually, always, often), and end with one line about the cash flow. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 2

1Good morning, everyone. Let me give you a quick update.Bom dia a todos. Deixem-me dar uma atualização rápida.
2I usually open with the cash flow, then move to operations.Eu geralmente começo pelo fluxo de caixa e depois vou para operações.
3I run operations and I lead a small team here.Eu comando as operações e lidero uma pequena equipe aqui.
4That's a good question. Let me check that and get back to you.Boa pergunta. Deixe-me verificar e te retorno.
5Just to bring everyone up to speed, here is where we stand.Só para atualizar todos, é aqui que estamos.
Aula 03 -- Pre-class

What Happened This Week -- Reporting Past Actions

Reporting last week in the Thursday meeting. Key words: close a deal, launch, hire, deliver, resolve, figure out, follow up on, roll out, revenue, quarter, shipment, outcome. Structures: simple past (regular & irregular), negatives with did not + base verb, and questions with did.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Close a deal -- to reach a final agreement on a sale or contract (fechar um negócio)
"Last week we closed a big deal with a new client."
Launch -- to start or introduce a new product or project (lançar)
"We launched the new product line last month."
Hire -- to give someone a job in your company (contratar)
"We hired two engineers last quarter."
Deliver -- to finish and hand over work on time (entregar)
"The team delivered the report ahead of schedule."
Resolve -- to find a solution to a problem (resolver)
"We resolved the customs issue last week."
Figure out -- to find the answer to a problem after thinking (decifrar / descobrir)
"We figured out the tax calculation yesterday."
Follow up on -- to check the progress of something later (dar seguimento a)
"I followed up on the invoice this morning."
Roll out -- to introduce something new step by step (implantar aos poucos)
"We rolled out the new tracking portal last month."
Revenue -- the money a business earns from sales (receita)
"Revenue grew by nine percent last quarter."
Quarter -- a three-month period in the business year (trimestre)
"It was a strong quarter for the company."
Shipment -- a load of goods sent to a customer (remessa / carga)
"The shipment arrived on Monday, right on time."
Outcome -- the final result of an action or meeting (resultado)
"The outcome of the meeting was very positive."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Close a deal
Launch
Hire
Deliver
Resolve
Figure out
Follow up on
Roll out
Revenue
Quarter
Shipment
Outcome

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

It was a busy week at AVRT. On Monday, Izabel closed the deal with the new supplier, and the first shipment arrived right on time. On Wednesday, the team resolved the customs issue, and Izabel followed up with the logistics team herself. She sent the invoices by email and delivered the cash-flow report by hand before the partners left on Friday. There was one open item: the team did not finish the tax restatement, because the new rules changed halfway through the quarter. At the Thursday call, a partner asked, "When did the shipment arrive?" and Izabel answered with a clear, confident recap.

1. Why do we write "closed", "arrived", and "resolved" with -ed?
A Because they are happening right now.
B Because they are regular verbs in the simple past.
C Because the subject is plural.
2. Look at "did not finish". Why is it "finish" and not "finished"?
A After "did not / didn't" we use the base verb, with no -ed.
B Because "finish" is always in the base form.
C Because the sentence is in the present.
3. The verbs "sent" and "left" do not end in -ed. Why?
A They are spelled wrong in the text.
B They are irregular verbs with a special past form (send→sent, leave→left).
C They are in the present simple.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Simple Past (Regular & Irregular)

GRAMMAR

Reporting finished actions in the past (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
Regular verb + -edAção terminada no passado: a maioria leva -edWe closed the deal.
Irregular (forma própria)Alguns verbos mudam: send→sent, leave→left, grow→grewI sent the report.
did not + baseNegativa: they/we/he did not (didn't) + verbo base (SEM -ed)We did not finish it.
(WH) + did + subject + basePergunta SEMPRE com did + sujeito + verbo baseWhat did you do?
Time markerslast week, yesterday, on Monday, two days ago, last quarter (tempo terminado — sem "since")

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct past form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Last week, we the deal."
"The shipment on Monday."
"I the report yesterday."
"We did not the tax restatement."
"When the partners leave?"
"Revenue nine percent last quarter."

Stage 2: Put the Recap in Order

Order

Put the events of last week in the order they happened.

?On Wednesday, we resolved the customs issue.
?On Friday, the partners left after the visit.
?On Monday, we closed the deal with the new supplier.
?On Thursday, I gave the recap on the weekly call.
?The same day, the first shipment arrived on time.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Last week we closed the deal and received the shipment.
Semana passada fechamos o negócio e recebemos a remessa.
The shipment arrived on Monday, right on time.
A remessa chegou na segunda, bem no prazo.
We didn't finish the tax restatement yet.
Ainda não terminamos a retificação tributária.
How did the last quarter go for your team?
Como foi o último trimestre para a sua equipe?
We rolled out the new tracking portal last month.
Implantamos o novo portal de rastreamento no mês passado.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

A partner asks what happened last week. Which recap is correct?
A "Last week we close the deal and the shipment arrive."
B "Last week we closed the deal and the shipment arrived."
C "Last week we are closing the deal and the shipment is arrive."
Which past negative is correct?
A "We didn't finished the tax restatement."
B "We didn't finish the tax restatement."
C "We not finished the tax restatement."
You want to ask a partner about the results. Which question is correct?
A "How did the last quarter go for your team?"
B "How the last quarter go for your team?"
C "How your team did go the last quarter?"
Which sentence uses the irregular past correctly?
A "I sent the invoices and the partners left on Friday."
B "I sended the invoices and the partners leaved on Friday."
C "I send the invoices and the partners leave on Friday."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give your weekly recap to the partners. Report three things that happened last week at AVRT (use the simple past), one thing you did not finish (did not + base verb), and end with one past question for the partners. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 3

1Last week, we closed the deal with the new supplier.Semana passada, fechamos o negócio com o novo fornecedor.
2The shipment arrived on Monday, right on time.A remessa chegou na segunda, bem no prazo.
3We resolved the customs issue on Wednesday.Resolvemos a questão alfandegária na quarta.
4We did not finish the tax restatement yet.Ainda não terminamos a retificação tributária.
5Revenue grew by nine percent last quarter.A receita cresceu nove por cento no último trimestre.
Aula 04 -- Pre-class

The Story So Far -- Talking About Progress

Reporting the company's journey with the present perfect. Key words: expand, scale up, invest in, improve, reach, achievement, headcount, benchmark, ramp up, break even, progress, so far. Structures: present perfect (has/have + past participle) vs simple past; since / for; already / yet / just.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Expand -- to grow bigger or add more (expandir / crescer)
"We have expanded the team since 2023."
Scale up -- to grow operations bigger and faster (ampliar a operação)
"We hired engineers to scale up production."
Invest in -- to put money into something to make it grow (investir em)
"AVRT has invested in new equipment."
Improve -- to make something better (melhorar)
"We have improved the reporting a lot."
Reach -- to arrive at a level, a target, or a place (alcançar / atingir)
"We have reached two new markets."
Achievement -- something done successfully, a result you are proud of (conquista)
"Breaking even was a real achievement."
Headcount -- the number of people a company employs (número de funcionários)
"Headcount has grown from twelve to twenty."
Benchmark -- a standard or target used to compare performance (referência / meta)
"We have hit almost every benchmark."
Ramp up -- to increase something quickly, like output (acelerar / aumentar)
"We have ramped up production this quarter."
Break even -- to reach the point where costs equal income (atingir o ponto de equilíbrio)
"We have broken even on the new line."
Progress -- forward movement; getting better over time (progresso)
"We have made strong progress this year."
So far -- up to now, until the present moment (até agora)
"So far, the results have been strong."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Expand
Scale up
Invest in
Improve
Reach
Achievement
Headcount
Benchmark
Ramp up
Break even
Progress
So far

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Over the last three years, AVRT has changed a great deal. When Izabel took over as CEO, the company was small. Since then, the team has expanded, revenue has grown every quarter, and the business has reached new markets in North America. AVRT has invested in new equipment and has broken even on the new product line. Izabel has not automated the reporting yet, so she still prepares some numbers by hand. Last week, she presented the year to the board, and for the first time she did it entirely in English. "We have come a long way," she says, "but we have not finished."

1. Why does the text say "has expanded", "has grown", and "has reached"?
A Because the actions happened at one exact finished time.
B Because they are changes up to now — present perfect (has + past participle).
C Because they are in the present simple.
2. "Last week, she presented the year to the board." Why simple past here?
A Because "last week" is a finished time, so we use the simple past.
B Because it is a change up to now.
C Because "present" has no past participle.
3. What has Izabel NOT done yet?
A She has not broken even.
B She has not automated the reporting yet.
C She has not reached any new markets.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Present Perfect vs Simple Past

GRAMMAR

Talking about change up to now vs a finished time (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
has / have + participleMudança ou resultado ATÉ AGORA (since, so far, just, already)The team has expanded.
have not + participle + yetAlgo ainda NÃO feitoWe have not finished it yet.
since / forsince + ponto inicial; for + duração. NUNCA "since the October"since January; for three years
Simple pastTempo TERMINADO: yesterday, last quarter, a year ago → She presented it yesterday.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Choose present perfect or simple past. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"Since 2023, the team expanded a lot."
"We reached two new markets."
"We have not the reporting yet."
"We even last quarter."
"She has her English a lot."
"We have waited October."

Stage 2: Put the Progress Story in Order

Order

Put the stages of AVRT's journey in the order they happened.

?We invested in new equipment and hired engineers.
?This year, we have broken even and reached new markets.
?Three years ago, the company was small and ran on spreadsheets.
?We scaled up production and improved the reporting.
?Izabel became CEO and started to lead the change.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

Since January, we have expanded the team and reached new markets.
Desde janeiro, expandimos a equipe e alcançamos novos mercados.
So far, the results have been strong.
Até agora, os resultados têm sido fortes.
We have broken even, but we haven't automated the reporting yet.
Atingimos o ponto de equilíbrio, mas ainda não automatizamos o relatório.
A year ago, everything ran on spreadsheets.
Um ano atrás, tudo funcionava em planilhas.
We have come a long way, but there is still work to do.
Percorremos um longo caminho, mas ainda há trabalho a fazer.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

A partner asks how the year has gone. Which recap is correct?
A "This year we expand the team and reach new markets."
B "This year we have expanded the team and reached new markets."
C "This year we have expand the team and have reach new markets."
Which sentence uses "since" correctly?
A "We wait since the October."
B "We have waited since October."
C "We have waited for October."
A finished time: which tense fits "Last quarter, we ___ even"?
A "broke even" (simple past)
B "have broken even" (present perfect)
C "have break even"
You want to say something is not done. Which is correct?
A "We have not automated the reporting yet."
B "We not automated the reporting yet."
C "We have not automate the reporting yet."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Present AVRT's year to the board. Say where the company started (simple past), three things it has achieved so far (present perfect), and one thing it has not finished yet. End with one line about your own progress in English. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 4

1Since January, we have expanded the team and reached new markets.Desde janeiro, expandimos a equipe e alcançamos novos mercados.
2So far, the results have been strong.Até agora, os resultados têm sido fortes.
3We have broken even, but we have not automated the reporting yet.Atingimos o equilíbrio, mas ainda não automatizamos o relatório.
4A year ago, everything ran on spreadsheets.Um ano atrás, tudo funcionava em planilhas.
5We have come a long way, but there is still work to do.Percorremos um longo caminho, mas ainda há trabalho a fazer.
Aula 05 -- Pre-class

What Do You Recommend? -- Making Recommendations

Giving recommendations in the Thursday meeting. Key words: recommend, suggest, advise, prioritize, postpone, delegate, reconsider, draft, outsource, cut costs, hold off on, weigh the options. Structures: modals of advice -- should, ought to, had better + base verb, and I would recommend / suggest + -ing.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Recommend -- to advise a course of action as the best choice (recomendar)
"Last week we closed a big deal, so I would recommend automating the reporting first."
Suggest -- to put an idea forward for people to consider (sugerir)
"I suggest we hold off on the launch for now."
Advise -- to give someone professional guidance (aconselhar)
"I would advise outsourcing the payroll."
Prioritize -- to deal with the most important things first (priorizar)
"We should prioritize the reporting system."
Postpone -- to move something to a later time (adiar)
"We might postpone the launch until July."
Delegate -- to give a task or responsibility to someone else (delegar)
"I recommend delegating the follow-ups to the team."
Reconsider -- to think about a decision again (reconsiderar)
"We ought to reconsider the timing."
Draft -- to prepare a first version of a plan or document (rascunhar (uma primeira versão))
"You should draft a twelve-month plan."
Outsource -- to pay an outside company to do work for you (terceirizar)
"It might be worth outsourcing the payroll."
Cut costs -- to reduce how much money you spend (cortar custos)
"We had better cut costs on the manual processes."
Hold off on -- to delay doing something until later (segurar / adiar (algo))
"I would suggest we hold off on that for one quarter."
Weigh the options -- to compare choices carefully before deciding (avaliar as opções)
"We ought to weigh the options before we invest."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Recommend
Suggest
Advise
Prioritize
Postpone
Delegate
Reconsider
Draft
Outsource
Cut costs
Hold off on
Weigh the options

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the Thursday call, the board asks Izabel for her recommendations. She is ready. "First, we should prioritize the reporting system," she says. "I would recommend automating it, because it still takes too much of my time." On the new product line, she is careful: "I suggest we hold off on the launch for one quarter. We ought to weigh the options before we invest more." On costs, her advice is direct: "We had better cut costs on the manual processes, and I would advise outsourcing the payroll." And on the team, she recommends patience: "I recommend delegating more to the current team first, and reconsidering the headcount after the audit."

1. Why is it "should prioritize" and not "should to prioritize"?
A Because "prioritize" is a special verb.
B After a modal like "should," we use the base verb with no "to."
C Because the sentence is a question.
2. Look at "I would recommend automating". Why "automating" and not "to automate"?
A "Recommend" and "suggest" are followed by -ing (or a "that" clause), never "to + verb".
B Because "automating" is a noun here.
C Because the action is in the past.
3. Which structure gives the strongest advice or warning?
A "I suggest we hold off on the launch."
B "We had better cut costs on the manual processes."
C "We ought to weigh the options."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modals of Advice & Recommendation

GRAMMAR

Giving recommendations in a meeting (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
should / shouldn't + baseConselho normal: modal + verbo base, SEM "to"You should review it.
ought to + baseConselho, um pouco mais formal (o "to" já faz parte)We ought to weigh the options.
had better + baseConselho forte / aviso: melhor fazer, SEM "to"We had better cut costs.
recommend / suggest + -ingRecomendação polida: NUNCA "recommend you to"I recommend automating it.
recommend / suggest (that) + baseForma mais formal: I recommend that you draft a plan.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"You prioritize the reporting."
"We to weigh the options first."
"We had cut costs before the audit."
"I would automating the report."
"I recommend a twelve-month plan."
"I suggest we off on the launch."

Stage 2: Put the Recommendations in Order

Order

Put Izabel's recommendations in a logical order, from first priority to last.

?Then, we had better cut costs and outsource the payroll.
?First, we should prioritize automating the reporting.
?Finally, we ought to reconsider the headcount after the audit.
?Next, I would suggest we hold off on the new launch.
?After that, I recommend delegating the follow-ups to the team.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

I would recommend automating the reporting first.
Eu recomendaria automatizar os relatórios primeiro.
We should prioritize the reporting system.
Deveríamos priorizar o sistema de relatórios.
We had better cut costs before the audit.
É melhor cortarmos custos antes da auditoria.
We ought to weigh the options carefully.
Deveríamos avaliar as opções com cuidado.
I would suggest we hold off on the launch.
Eu sugeriria que segurássemos o lançamento.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

A partner asks what to focus on first. Which recommendation is correct?
A "You should to prioritize the reporting."
B "You should prioritize the reporting."
C "You should prioritizing the reporting."
Which recommendation with "recommend" is correct?
A "I recommend you to outsource the payroll."
B "I recommend outsourcing the payroll."
C "I recommend to outsource the payroll."
You want to give a strong warning about costs. Which is best?
A "We had better cut costs before the audit."
B "We had better to cut costs before the audit."
C "We had better cutting costs before the audit."
Which sentence advises waiting on a decision?
A "I suggest we hold off on the launch and reconsider the timing."
B "I suggest we hold off on the launch and reconsidering the timing."
C "I suggest to hold off the launch and to reconsider the timing."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Give the board your recommendations for AVRT next quarter. Recommend three priorities (use should, ought to, and had better), say one thing you would advise holding off on, and end with one question for the partners. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 5

1I would recommend automating the reporting first.Eu recomendaria automatizar os relatórios primeiro.
2We should prioritize the audit before we hire.Deveríamos priorizar a auditoria antes de contratar.
3We ought to weigh the options carefully.Deveríamos avaliar as opções com cuidado.
4We had better cut costs on the manual processes.É melhor cortarmos custos nos processos manuais.
5I would suggest we hold off on the launch for one quarter.Eu sugeriria segurar o lançamento por um trimestre.
Aula 06 -- Pre-class

How AVRT Works -- Describing Processes with the Passive

Describing how AVRT makes, checks, and reports. Key words: manufacture, assemble, inspect, certify, approve, dispatch, monitor, audit, submit, store, label, review. Structure: the passive voice -- be (is/are/was/were/has been) + past participle.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Manufacture -- to make products in a factory (fabricar)
"The units are manufactured in Valinhos."
Assemble -- to put parts together into a finished product (montar)
"The parts are assembled on site."
Inspect -- to examine something carefully for quality (inspecionar)
"Each unit is inspected before shipping."
Certify -- to officially confirm something meets a standard (certificar)
"The product is certified to a strict standard."
Approve -- to officially accept a decision or document (aprovar)
"The batch is approved for dispatch."
Dispatch -- to send goods out to customers (expedir / despachar)
"The orders are dispatched to North America."
Monitor -- to watch and check something over time (monitorar)
"Every shipment is monitored to the client."
Audit -- to officially examine financial records (auditar)
"The accounts are audited every quarter."
Submit -- to send a document to someone in authority (submeter / enviar)
"The report is submitted to the board."
Store -- to keep data or goods safely (armazenar)
"The delivery data is stored in one system."
Label -- to attach information to a product (rotular / etiquetar)
"Each order is labeled before packing."
Review -- to examine something to check it is correct (revisar)
"The figures are reviewed by Izabel."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each verb with the correct definition.

Manufacture
Assemble
Inspect
Certify
Approve
Dispatch
Monitor
Audit
Submit
Store
Label
Review

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At AVRT, every component follows a careful process. The parts are designed in-house, and the units are manufactured in Valinhos. Each batch is inspected by the quality team, and once a product is certified, it is approved for shipping. The orders are labeled and dispatched to North America, and every shipment is monitored to the client. Each quarter, the accounts are audited by an external firm. The report is reviewed by Izabel, and once the figures are confirmed, it is submitted to the board. Last week, the full report was submitted on time, and this quarter every account has been audited already.

1. Why do we say "the units are manufactured" and not "the units manufacture"?
A Because "units" is plural.
B It is passive: the units receive the action, so we use be + past participle.
C Because it is a question.
2. Look at "has been audited". Why is "been" there?
A The present perfect passive is has/have + been + past participle.
B "been" is a spelling mistake.
C Because the action is in the future.
3. Why does the writer use the passive so much in this text?
A Because the doer is always unknown.
B Because the process and the steps matter more than who does each one.
C Because the passive is shorter than the active.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Passive Voice

GRAMMAR

Describing a process when the action matters more than the doer (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
is / are + past participlePassivo no presente: ação habitual; quem faz não importaThe units are manufactured.
was / were + past participlePassivo no passado: ação terminadaThe report was submitted.
has / have been + past participlePassivo no present perfect: resultado até agora (NÃO esqueça "been")The accounts have been audited.
by + agente (opcional)Só quando o autor importa: inspected by the quality team.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence with the correct passive form. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The components in Valinhos."
"Each unit before shipping."
"Yesterday, the report to the board."
"The accounts this quarter."
"Every order to North America."
"The delivery data in one system."

Stage 2: Put the Process in Order

Order

Put the steps of the AVRT process in the order they happen.

?Once it is certified, the product is approved for shipping.
?First, the units are manufactured in Valinhos.
?Finally, the report is reviewed and submitted to the board.
?Then each batch is inspected by the quality team.
?After that, the orders are dispatched and monitored to the client.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

The components are manufactured in Valinhos and inspected on site.
Os componentes são fabricados em Valinhos e inspecionados no local.
Once a product is certified, it is approved for shipping.
Quando um produto é certificado, ele é aprovado para envio.
The report was submitted to the board on time.
O relatório foi enviado ao conselho no prazo.
The accounts have been audited by an external firm.
As contas foram auditadas por uma empresa externa.
Every shipment is monitored and stored in the system.
Cada remessa é monitorada e armazenada no sistema.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

A partner asks where the products are made. Which answer is correct?
A "The units are manufacture in Valinhos."
B "The units are manufactured in Valinhos."
C "The units is manufactured in Valinhos."
Which present perfect passive is correct?
A "The accounts have audited this quarter."
B "The accounts have been audited this quarter."
C "The accounts has been audit this quarter."
You want to say a finished action in the past passive. Which is correct?
A "The report was submitted yesterday."
B "The report was submit yesterday."
C "The report submitted yesterday."
When should you add "by + the quality team"?
A Only when it is important to say who does the action.
B Always, in every passive sentence.
C Never -- the passive cannot name the doer.

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Describe how a product is made and shipped at AVRT, from the factory to the client. Use the passive voice: how it is manufactured, inspected, certified, and dispatched. Then say how the report is audited, reviewed, and submitted to the board. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 6

1The components are manufactured in Valinhos and inspected on site.Os componentes são fabricados em Valinhos e inspecionados no local.
2Once a product is certified, it is approved for shipping.Quando um produto é certificado, ele é aprovado para envio.
3Every order is labeled, dispatched, and monitored.Cada pedido é rotulado, expedido e monitorado.
4The accounts are audited every quarter.As contas são auditadas a cada trimestre.
5The report is reviewed and then submitted to the board.O relatório é revisado e depois enviado ao conselho.
Aula 07 -- Pre-class

The Road Ahead -- Talking About Plans and the Future

Presenting your plan for the next quarter. Key words: roadmap, timeline, forecast, projection, target, allocate, kick off, scope, pipeline, phase, contingency, anticipate. Structures: the three future forms -- will (instant decisions / predictions), be going to (decided plans / evidence-based forecasts), and present continuous (fixed arrangements).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Roadmap -- a plan showing how something will develop over time (plano / roteiro)
"Last class you described how AVRT works. Now I will bring a clear roadmap for the next quarter."
Timeline -- the schedule showing when each stage happens (cronograma)
"The timeline is set: the line launches in April."
Forecast -- a statement of what is likely to happen in the future (previsão)
"Our forecast is a fifteen percent rise in revenue."
Projection -- an estimate of future numbers (projeção)
"The projection for the new line is very promising."
Target -- to aim for a specific result (mirar / ter como meta)
"We are targeting twelve new accounts by December."
Allocate -- to assign money or resources to a purpose (alocar / destinar)
"We are going to allocate more budget to research."
Kick off -- to start a project or activity (dar início a algo)
"The first phase kicks off in early April."
Scope -- the range of what a project covers (escopo / abrangência)
"First, let us agree on the scope of the new market."
Pipeline -- projects or products being developed for the future (carteira de projetos em desenvolvimento)
"More research keeps the pipeline full."
Phase -- a distinct stage in a plan (fase / etapa)
"The first phase is the pilot; the second is the full launch."
Contingency -- a backup plan for if something goes wrong (plano de contingência)
"We always keep a contingency if a supplier is late."
Anticipate -- to expect something and prepare for it (antecipar-se / prever e se preparar)
"Anticipate the questions the board will ask."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Roadmap
Timeline
Forecast
Projection
Target
Allocate
Kick off
Scope
Pipeline
Phase
Contingency
Anticipate

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the Thursday call, the board asks Izabel for the road ahead. She is ready. "In the third quarter, we are launching the new wireless sensor line," she says. "The timeline is set, and the first phase kicks off in April." On the numbers, she is confident: "Our forecast is strong. We are going to grow revenue by about fifteen percent, and the projection for the new line is very promising." A partner asks about research. "We are going to allocate more budget to it, so the pipeline stays full. I will send you the full roadmap after the call." Finally, on risk: "We always keep a contingency, and I am meeting the regional partners next Tuesday to agree on the scope."

1. She says "we are launching the new line in Q3." Why the present continuous?
A Because it is happening right now, as she speaks.
B It is a fixed arrangement for the future (present continuous = am/is/are + -ing).
C Because the action is a habit.
2. Look at "we are going to grow revenue." Why "going to" and not "will"?
A It is a plan already decided / a prediction based on evidence, so we use "be going to".
B Because "will" is never used for the future.
C Because "grow" cannot follow "will".
3. Which sentence is an instant decision made at the moment of speaking?
A "We are meeting the regional partners next Tuesday."
B "I will send you the full roadmap after the call."
C "We are going to allocate more budget to research."

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Talking About the Future

GRAMMAR

The three future forms (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
will + baseDecisão na hora, oferta ou previsão sem prova concretaI will send it tonight.
be going to + basePlano JÁ decidido, ou previsão com base em evidênciaWe are going to launch in Q3.
am / is / are + -ingCompromisso marcado (arranjo fixo), geralmente com data/horaWe are meeting them on Tuesday.
Cuidado / Watch outNão use o presente simples para arranjos: errado "I meet them Tuesday" → certo "I am meeting them on Tuesday".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We are to allocate more budget to research."
"I send you the forecast tonight."
"We are the partners on Tuesday."
"The first phase off in early April."
"Our is a fifteen percent rise in revenue."
"We always keep a if a supplier is late."

Stage 2: Put the Roadmap in Order

Order

Put Izabel's plan in a logical order, from the first step to the last.

?Then, we are going to grow revenue by about fifteen percent.
?First, the first phase kicks off in early April.
?Finally, I will send the full roadmap after the call.
?Next, we are launching the new sensor line in the third quarter.
?After that, we are going to allocate more budget to research.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

We are launching the new sensor line in the third quarter.
Vamos lançar a nova linha de sensores no terceiro trimestre.
We are going to grow revenue by about fifteen percent.
Vamos aumentar a receita em cerca de quinze por cento.
The first phase kicks off in early April.
A primeira fase começa no início de abril.
We are meeting the partners on Tuesday.
Vamos nos reunir com os sócios na terça-feira.
I will send you the full roadmap after the call.
Vou te enviar o roadmap completo depois da ligação.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You have a fixed meeting with the partners next Tuesday. How do you say it?
A "I meet the partners next Tuesday."
B "I am meeting the partners next Tuesday."
C "I will meeting the partners next Tuesday."
The launch is a plan you already decided. Which is correct?
A "We are going to launch the new line in Q3."
B "We will launching the new line in Q3."
C "We are going to launching the new line in Q3."
A partner asks for the plan and you decide, right now, to send it. Which is best?
A "Sure, I will send you the roadmap tonight."
B "Sure, I send you the roadmap tonight."
C "Sure, I am going to sending you the roadmap tonight."
Which sentence talks about the numbers with an evidence-based forecast?
A "Revenue is going to grow by about fifteen percent."
B "Revenue grows by about fifteen percent next year."
C "Revenue will to grow by about fifteen percent."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Present your plan for AVRT next quarter. Say what you are launching and when (present continuous), what you are going to do with the budget and the numbers (going to), and one thing you will decide on the spot (will). End with your contingency for risk. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 7

1We are launching the new sensor line in the third quarter.Vamos lançar a nova linha de sensores no terceiro trimestre.
2We are going to grow revenue by about fifteen percent.Vamos aumentar a receita em cerca de quinze por cento.
3The first phase kicks off in early April.A primeira fase começa no início de abril.
4I will send you the full roadmap after the call.Vou te enviar o roadmap completo depois da ligação.
5We always keep a contingency in case a supplier is late.Sempre mantemos um plano de contingência caso um fornecedor atrase.
Aula 08 -- Pre-class

The Market Outlook -- Conditions, Scenarios and the First Conditional

Reading a market outlook and planning for each scenario. Key words: demand, competitor, capacity, margin, downturn, surge, volatile, diversify, disruption, break into, lead time, market share. Structure: the first conditional -- if + present simple, ... will + base verb; plus "unless" (= if not) and "as long as".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Demand -- how much customers want to buy a product (demanda / procura)
"Last class you shared your roadmap; now the market matters. Demand can surge in one quarter and cool the next."
Competitor -- a company that sells similar products in the same market (concorrente)
"A larger competitor may enter the same segment."
Capacity -- the maximum a company can produce (capacidade produtiva)
"If demand rises, we will expand our capacity."
Margin -- the profit left after costs (margem de lucro)
"If costs go up, our margin will fall."
Downturn -- a period when business or the economy gets weaker (queda / retração)
"If a downturn hits, costs will be cut."
Surge -- a sudden large increase (disparada / forte aumento)
"A surge in demand can double our orders overnight."
Volatile -- changing quickly and unpredictably (volátil / instável)
"The market is strong but volatile."
Diversify -- to add new products or markets to reduce risk (diversificar)
"We will diversify to reduce risk."
Disruption -- a major change that interrupts the normal way of doing things (disrupção / ruptura)
"A new competitor could be a real disruption."
Break into -- to enter a new market successfully (entrar em um novo mercado)
"If demand rises, we will break into North America."
Lead time -- the time between ordering and delivering (prazo de entrega)
"We compete on quality and lead time, not price."
Market share -- the portion of a market that a company controls (participação de mercado)
"We will grow our market share step by step."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Demand
Competitor
Capacity
Margin
Downturn
Surge
Volatile
Diversify
Disruption
Break into
Lead time
Market share

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

At the Thursday call, Izabel presents the market outlook. It is strong but volatile, so she has a plan for each scenario. "If demand keeps rising, we will expand our capacity and break into North America," she says. "If a big competitor enters, we will not compete on price; we will compete on quality and lead time." On risk, she is direct: "If a downturn hits, costs will be cut and the launch will be postponed, not cancelled. Unless the market collapses, we will keep investing in research." And on growth: "As long as the core line stays strong, we will diversify and grow our market share."

1. Why is it "if demand keeps rising" and not "if demand will keep rising"?
A Because "demand" cannot take "will".
B In the first conditional, the "if" part uses the present simple, never "will".
C Because the sentence is about the past.
2. What does "Unless the market collapses" mean?
A "If the market does not collapse" -- "unless" already means "if not".
B "Because the market collapses."
C "After the market collapses."
3. In "if a downturn hits, costs will be cut," which part is the result?
A "if a downturn hits" (present simple).
B "costs will be cut" -- the result uses "will".
C Both parts use "will".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The First Conditional

GRAMMAR

Talking about a real future that depends on a condition (explicação em inglês e português).

PartForm / UsoExample
if / when + condiçãoPresent simple — NUNCA "will" depois de "if"If demand rises...
resultadowill + verbo base — a consequência futura... we will expand.
unless= "if not" (não use outra negação junto)Unless we act, they win.
may / mightUse no resultado quando é só possível: If demand cools, we might postpone the launch.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If demand , we will expand our capacity."
"If a competitor , we will compete on quality."
"If a downturn hits, costs be cut."
" the market collapses, we will keep investing."
"As long as the core line stays strong, we will ."
"If demand cools, we postpone the launch."

Stage 2: Put the Scenario Plan in Order

Order

Put Izabel's scenario plan in a logical order, from the best case to the worst case.

?If a competitor enters, we will compete on quality, not price.
?If demand rises, we will expand our capacity and break into North America.
?If a downturn hits, costs will be cut and the launch will be postponed.
?As long as the core line stays strong, we will diversify and grow our market share.
?Unless the whole market collapses, we will keep investing in research.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If demand rises, we will expand our capacity.
Se a demanda subir, vamos ampliar nossa capacidade.
If a competitor enters, we will compete on quality, not price.
Se um concorrente entrar, vamos competir em qualidade, não em preço.
If a downturn hits, costs will be cut.
Se houver uma retração, os custos serão cortados.
Unless the market collapses, we will keep investing.
A não ser que o mercado desabe, vamos continuar investindo.
As long as the core line stays strong, we will diversify.
Enquanto a linha principal continuar forte, vamos diversificar.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

A partner asks what you will do if demand rises. Which is correct?
A "If demand will rise, we will expand our capacity."
B "If demand rises, we will expand our capacity."
C "If demand rises, we expand our capacity next year."
Which sentence uses "unless" correctly?
A "Unless we act now, a competitor will break into the market first."
B "Unless we don't act now, a competitor will break into the market first."
C "Unless we will act now, a competitor breaks into the market first."
You want to say a result is only possible, not certain. Which is best?
A "If demand cools, we might postpone the launch."
B "If demand will cool, we postpone the launch."
C "If demand cools, we will might postpone the launch."
A partner worries about a downturn. Which reassures them best?
A "If a downturn hits, costs will be cut and the launch postponed, not cancelled."
B "If a downturn will hit, we cutting costs."
C "If a downturn hits, we cancelling everything."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Present AVRT's plan for the market to the board. Give three first-conditional sentences: one for if demand rises, one for if a competitor enters, and one for if a downturn hits. Use "unless" or "as long as" at least once, and end with one question for the partners. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 8

1If demand rises, we will expand our capacity and break into North America.Se a demanda subir, vamos ampliar a capacidade e entrar na América do Norte.
2If a competitor enters, we will compete on quality, not price.Se um concorrente entrar, vamos competir em qualidade, não em preço.
3If a downturn hits, costs will be cut and the launch postponed.Se houver uma retração, os custos serão cortados e o lançamento adiado.
4Unless the market collapses, we will keep investing in research.A não ser que o mercado desabe, vamos continuar investindo em pesquisa.
5As long as the core line stays strong, we will diversify.Enquanto a linha principal continuar forte, vamos diversificar.
Aula 09 -- Pre-class

The What-If Room -- Weighing Hypotheticals with the Second Conditional

Thinking in hypotheticals: what AVRT would do if an unlikely scenario happened. Key words: trade-off, bottleneck, overhead, incentive, worst-case scenario, hedge, feasible, leverage, concession, bargaining chip, undercut, walk away. Structure: the second conditional -- if + past simple, would/could + base verb -- for unreal, hypothetical situations (If we had more capacity, we would take the contract).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Trade-off -- a balance where you give up one thing to gain another (compromisso / troca)
"Last class you planned for likely conditions. Today, every option is a trade-off between speed and cost."
Bottleneck -- a point that slows everything down and limits output (gargalo)
"If we took the whole contract, it would create a bottleneck in production."
Overhead -- the ongoing fixed costs of running a business (custos fixos / despesas gerais)
"If we opened a second plant, our overhead would rise sharply."
Incentive -- something that motivates a person to act (incentivo)
"If we offered a small incentive, the buyer would commit sooner."
Worst-case scenario -- the most unfavorable possible outcome (pior cenário possível)
"In the worst-case scenario, we would lose our biggest account."
Hedge -- to protect yourself against loss or risk (proteger-se / mitigar risco)
"I would hedge: take half the volume and keep the rest of our capacity free."
Feasible -- possible to do successfully; realistic (viável / factível)
"A second plant would only be feasible if the volume held for two years."
Leverage -- the power to influence a negotiation in your favor (poder de barganha / alavancagem)
"If I sold now, I would lose my leverage over the roadmap."
Concession -- something you give up to reach an agreement (concessão)
"If they made one concession on delivery, I would sign."
Bargaining chip -- something valuable you use to gain an advantage in a deal (moeda de troca / trunfo)
"Our lead time would be my strongest bargaining chip."
Undercut -- to sell at a lower price than a competitor (praticar preço mais baixo que)
"If a rival undercut us, we would not follow them down."
Walk away -- to leave a negotiation without making a deal (sair da mesa / desistir do acordo)
"If the terms were not right, I would walk away."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Trade-off
Bottleneck
Overhead
Incentive
Worst-case scenario
Hedge
Feasible
Leverage
Concession
Bargaining chip
Undercut
Walk away

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andy opens the strategy session with a what-if drill. "Suppose that global buyer offered you the big contract," he says. Izabel is careful. "If we took the whole contract, it would create a bottleneck in production," she explains. "But if they made one concession on delivery, I would sign for half the volume. Our lead time would be my strongest bargaining chip." On competition, she is just as clear: "If a rival undercut us, we would not follow them down. If we cut our price to match, our overhead would eat the margin, so we would compete on quality." And the acquisition offer? "If I were in their position, I would wait. Every option is a trade-off. If the terms were not right, I would walk away."

1. "If we took the whole contract, it would create a bottleneck." Is Izabel describing something real or hypothetical?
A Something real that is happening now.
B A hypothetical, unreal situation (second conditional: if + past simple, would + base).
C A finished action in the past.
2. Look at "If a rival undercut us, we would not follow." Why "would" and not "will"?
A The situation is imagined / unlikely, so the result uses "would + base verb".
B Because "will" is never correct in English.
C Because "follow" cannot go with "will".
3. Izabel says "If I were in their position..." Why "were" and not "was"?
A Because "I was" is grammatically impossible anywhere.
B In hypotheticals we use "were" for every subject (If I were, if he were).
C Because the sentence is about the past.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Second Conditional

GRAMMAR

The hypothetical conditional (explicação em inglês e português).

FormUse / UsoExample
Afirmativa: If + past simple, would + baseSituação hipotética / irreal no presente ou futuroIf we had more capacity, we would take it.
Negativa: ..., would not (wouldn't) + baseResultado imaginado que NÃO aconteceriaIf a rival undercut us, we would not follow.
Interrogativa: What would you do if...?Perguntar sobre um cenário hipotéticoWhat would you do if they offered more?
Verbo "be": were p/ todosHipotético formal: use "were", não "was"If I were you, I would wait.
Cuidado / Watch outNUNCA use "would" depois de "if": errado "If we would have..." → certo "If we had...". O resultado usa would, não will.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If we the whole contract, it would create a bottleneck."
"If a rival undercut us, we compete on quality."
"If I you, I would walk away."
"If they made one on delivery, I would sign."
"If we opened a second plant, our would rise sharply."
"If the terms were not right, I would away."

Stage 2: Put the Reasoning in Order

Order

Put Izabel's what-if reasoning in a logical order, from the opening to the final decision.

?If they made one concession on delivery, I would sign for half the volume.
?First, let us imagine the big contract came in full.
?And in the worst-case scenario, if the terms were not right, I would walk away.
?If we took the whole thing, it would create a bottleneck in production.
?Then, if a rival undercut us, we would compete on quality, not price.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If we took the whole contract, it would create a bottleneck.
Se aceitássemos o contrato inteiro, criaria um gargalo.
If a rival undercut us, we would compete on quality.
Se um concorrente baixasse o preço, competiríamos na qualidade.
If I were you, I would walk away.
Se eu fosse você, eu sairia da mesa.
If they made one concession, I would sign today.
Se eles fizessem uma concessão, eu assinaria hoje.
If we opened a second plant, our overhead would rise.
Se abríssemos uma segunda fábrica, nossos custos fixos subiriam.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You want to say what would happen (hypothetically) if you had more capacity. Which is correct?
A "If we would have more capacity, we would take the contract."
B "If we had more capacity, we would take the contract."
C "If we have more capacity, we would take the contract."
A rival might undercut you. How do you describe your hypothetical response?
A "If a rival undercut us, we would compete on quality."
B "If a rival undercut us, we will compete on quality."
C "If a rival would undercut us, we compete on quality."
You want to give advice as if you were in the buyer's position. Which is best?
A "If I were the buyer, I would ask for a concession."
B "If I was the buyer, I will ask for a concession."
C "If I am the buyer, I would asking for a concession."
Which sentence correctly imagines a bad outcome of matching a rival's price?
A "If we cut our price to match, our margin would collapse."
B "If we cut our price to match, our margin will collapsing."
C "If we would cut our price to match, our margin collapse."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine three hypotheticals for AVRT: what you would do if the big contract came in full, if a rival undercut you on price, and if a group offered to acquire the company. Use the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base), and name at least one trade-off. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 9

1If we took the whole contract, it would create a bottleneck.Se aceitássemos o contrato inteiro, criaria um gargalo.
2If they made one concession on delivery, I would sign.Se eles fizessem uma concessão na entrega, eu assinaria.
3If a rival undercut us, we would compete on quality, not price.Se um concorrente baixasse o preço, competiríamos na qualidade, não no preço.
4If I were you, I would treat the timeline as a bargaining chip.Se eu fosse você, eu usaria o prazo como moeda de troca.
5If the terms were not right, I would walk away.Se os termos não estivessem certos, eu sairia da mesa.
Aula 10 -- Pre-class

The Meeting, Relayed -- Reporting What Was Said

Reading the minutes of a board meeting and relaying what was said. Key words: agenda, minutes, recap, brief, clarify, endorse, defer, action item, takeaway, circulate, debrief, consensus. Structure: reported speech -- backshift (present -> past, will -> would, can -> could), say vs tell, reported questions with "if / whether", and reported commands with "told + someone + to".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Agenda -- the list of topics a meeting will discuss (pauta / agenda da reunião)
"The client was next on the agenda."
Minutes -- the official written record of a meeting (ata da reunião)
"Izabel circulated the minutes after the meeting."
Recap -- a short summary of the main points (resumo / retomada)
"Grace gave a quick recap of Thursday's meeting."
Brief -- to give someone the key information they need (informar / dar um briefing)
"The CFO briefed the board on the numbers."
Clarify -- to make something clearer or remove confusion (esclarecer)
"The board agreed to clarify the delivery date."
Endorse -- to give official or public support to something (endossar / apoiar oficialmente)
"The board decided to endorse the launch."
Defer -- to postpone a decision to a later time (adiar / postergar)
"The board chose to defer the second plant."
Action item -- a task assigned to someone after a meeting (tarefa / ação definida na reunião)
"Izabel assigned two action items."
Takeaway -- the most important point to remember (principal lição / ponto-chave)
"The key takeaway was to confirm the numbers first."
Circulate -- to send a document around to a group of people (distribuir / fazer circular)
"She asked finance to circulate an updated forecast."
Debrief -- to review and discuss an event after it happens (fazer um balanço / relato p&243;s-evento)
"Oscar called with a quick debrief after the client call."
Consensus -- general agreement among a group (consenso)
"The board reached a consensus on the launch."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Agenda
Minutes
Recap
Brief
Clarify
Endorse
Defer
Action item
Takeaway
Circulate
Debrief
Consensus

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

After Thursday's board meeting, Izabel relays it to a partner who was away. The chair said that the year had been strong, and he explained that the sensor business had grown faster than expected. The CFO reported that the margin had improved, but she warned that overhead would rise with a second plant. A client asked whether AVRT could shorten its lead time. In the end, Izabel told the operations lead to review the schedule, and the board reached a consensus to endorse the launch.

1. Why is it "the year had been strong" and not "the year has been strong"?
A Because "year" is always in the past.
B In reported speech the verb shifts back one tense (backshift): has been -> had been.
C Because the chair spoke very slowly.
2. The client "asked whether AVRT could shorten its lead time." Why "could", not "can"?
A In reported questions, "can" shifts back to "could".
B "Can" is never used in business English.
C Because the client was unsure.
3. Why "told the operations lead to review" and not "said the operations lead to review"?
A "Said" and "told" are always interchangeable.
B "Tell" takes a person (told + someone); "say" does not. Reported commands use "told + someone + to + base".
C Because "review" starts with an R.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Reported Speech

GRAMMAR

Relaying what someone said (explicação em inglês e português).

DirectReported / UsoExample
present -> pastbackshift: is/are -> was/were; has -> hadShe said the margin had improved.
will / can -> would / couldo modal recua um passoHe said they would sign.
say vs telltell + pessoa; say (to) SEM pessoa — NUNCA "said me"She told me; he said that...
reported question / commandask if / whether (sem inversão, sem "do"): She asked whether we could deliver. Comando: told + sb + to + base.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"The chair said that the results strong."
"She me that the margin had improved."
"The client asked we could shorten the lead time."
"He explained that they endorse the launch."
"Izabel told the operations lead review the schedule."
"The board reached a to endorse the launch."

Stage 2: Put the Relayed Meeting in Order

Order

Put Izabel's relay of the meeting in a logical order, from the opening to the final decision.

?A client asked whether we could shorten the lead time.
?The chair said that the year had been strong.
?The board reached a consensus to endorse the launch but defer the plant.
?The CFO reported that the margin had improved.
?Izabel told the operations lead to review the schedule.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

The chair said that the results had been strong.
O presidente disse que os resultados tinham sido fortes.
She told the board that the margin had improved.
Ela disse ao conselho que a margem tinha melhorado.
The client asked whether we could shorten the lead time.
O cliente perguntou se poderíamos reduzir o prazo de entrega.
I told the operations lead to review the schedule.
Eu disse ao líder de operações para revisar o cronograma.
The board reached a consensus to endorse the launch.
O conselho chegou a um consenso para endossar o lançamento.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

A partner asks what the CFO said. Which is correct?
A "She said me that the margin has improved."
B "She told me that the margin had improved."
C "She said me the margin improved."
Which relays a client's question correctly?
A "The client asked whether we could shorten the lead time."
B "The client asked can we shorten the lead time."
C "The client asked do we can shorten the lead time."
You want to relay a command you gave. Which is best?
A "I told the operations lead to review the schedule."
B "I said the operations lead review the schedule."
C "I told to the operations lead review the schedule."
The chair said, "The launch is approved." How do you relay it later?
A "The chair said that the launch was approved."
B "The chair said that the launch is approved."
C "The chair told that the launch was approved."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Relay your last real meeting to a partner who missed it. Report at least three things people said, using said / told / explained / reported and correct backshift. Include one reported question ("asked whether...") and one reported command ("told someone to..."). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 10

1The chair said that the results had been strong.O presidente disse que os resultados tinham sido fortes.
2She told the board that the margin had improved.Ela disse ao conselho que a margem tinha melhorado.
3The client asked whether we could shorten the lead time.O cliente perguntou se poderíamos reduzir o prazo de entrega.
4I told the operations lead to review the schedule.Eu disse ao líder de operações para revisar o cronograma.
5The board reached a consensus to endorse the launch.O conselho chegou a um consenso para endossar o lançamento.
Aula 11 -- Pre-class

Reading the Signals -- Interpreting What Must Have Happened

Interpreting ambiguous business signals: a client who goes quiet, a late shipment, a rival that cuts its price. Key words: infer, assumption, speculate, red flag, gut feeling, plausible, rule out, jump to conclusions, read between the lines, far-fetched, stall, hold back. Structure: modals of deduction -- must / might / could / can't + be (now) and + have + past participle (past) -- to say how sure you are (They must be busy; they cannot have signed yet).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Infer -- to conclude something from evidence, not from a direct statement (inferir / deduzir)
"From the warm tone of the email, I infer that the client is still interested."
Assumption -- something you accept as true without proof (suposição / premissa)
"My assumption is that they lost a major client last quarter."
Speculate -- to form a theory without firm evidence (especular)
"I do not want to speculate too far, but they might be comparing prices."
Red flag -- a clear warning sign of a problem (sinal de alerta)
"Two weeks of silence after a strong meeting is a red flag."
Gut feeling -- an instinct or hunch, not based on hard facts (intuição / palpite)
"My gut feeling is that they are stalling to gain leverage."
Plausible -- reasonable and likely to be true (plausível)
"A scheduling delay is the most plausible explanation for the silence."
Rule out -- to eliminate something as a possibility (descartar)
"I would rule out the worst case: they cannot have signed yet."
Jump to conclusions -- to judge before you have the facts (tirar conclusões precipitadas)
"Let us not jump to conclusions after only two weeks of silence."
Read between the lines -- to understand a meaning that is not stated openly (ler nas entrelinhas)
"If I read between the lines, the tone was warmer than the silence suggests."
Far-fetched -- unlikely; hard to believe (improvável / forçado)
"The idea that this price cut is a real long-term threat is far-fetched."
Stall -- to delay deliberately to gain time (enrolar / ganhar tempo)
"They might be stalling to gain a little leverage before they answer."
Hold back -- to keep information or a decision to yourself (segurar / não revelar)
"Do not hold back your own story: your quality and lead time still win."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Infer
Assumption
Speculate
Red flag
Gut feeling
Plausible
Rule out
Jump to conclusions
