Read between the lines
Far-fetched
Stall
Hold back

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andy is worried. The client has gone quiet for two weeks. "Let us not jump to conclusions," Izabel says. "They replied warmly last week, so they must still be interested. Their procurement lead was traveling, so the delay could just be a scheduling thing." Andy raises a red flag: a colleague saw the client at a supplier fair. "Then they might be comparing prices," Izabel admits, "but they cannot have signed with anyone yet, because our quote is still open." On the rival that suddenly cut its price, she is just as careful. "That price is below cost, so they cannot be making money on it. They must have lost a big account, and this might be a defensive move." Her read: "They must be stalling for leverage. I would rule out the worst case."

1. "They must still be interested." How sure is Izabel?
A Almost certain, based on the evidence (must = strong deduction).
B Just one weak possibility among many.
C She thinks it is impossible.
2. "They cannot have signed with anyone yet." What does this mean?
A They probably signed with a rival.
B It is impossible that they signed (deduction about the past: can't have + participle).
C They are not allowed to sign.
3. "It could just be a scheduling thing." Why "could be" and not "must be"?
A It is only one possibility, not a certainty (could / might = possible).
B Because "must be" is always wrong.
C Because the sentence is about the past.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Modals of Deduction

GRAMMAR

Saying how sure you are (explicação em inglês e português).

Certainty / CertezaNow / AgoraPast / Passado
Certo (~95%)must + be/base
They must be busy.		must have + particípio
They must have left.
Possível (~50%)might / could / may + be/base
It might be a delay.		might / could / may have + particípio
She could have missed it.
Impossível (~0%)cannot (can't) + be/base
They cannot be serious.		cannot (can't) have + particípio
They cannot have signed.
Cuidado / Watch outNunca use "to" depois do modal: errado "must to be" → certo "must be". Use o particípio depois de "have": "must have gone" (não "go"). Para o passado impossível use can't have, não "mustn't have".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"They replied last night, so they be busy, not gone."
"It be a simple scheduling delay."
"Our quote is still open, so they have signed yet."
"The price is below cost, so they must have a major account."
"She never opened it, so she could have the message."
"My gut is that they are stalling for leverage."

Stage 2: Put the Read-Out in Order

Order

Put Izabel's read-out in a logical order, from the first signal to the final decision.

?So I would rule out the worst case and simply call them Thursday.
?They replied warmly before, so they must still be interested.
?First, the client has been silent for two weeks after a strong meeting.
?But our quote is still open, so they cannot have signed with anyone yet.
?Their team was seen at a supplier fair, so they might be comparing prices.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

They must be busy, not gone.
Eles devem estar ocupados, não sumidos.
They must have found a cheaper supplier.
Eles devem ter achado um fornecedor mais barato.
It might be a simple scheduling delay.
Pode ser um simples atraso de agenda.
They cannot have signed with anyone yet.
É impossível que já tenham assinado com alguém.
Let us not jump to conclusions.
Vamos não tirar conclusões precipitadas.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You are almost certain, from the evidence, that the client is still interested. Which is correct?
A "They must to be still interested."
B "They must be still interested."
C "They must being still interested."
It is impossible that they signed, because your quote is still open. Which is best?
A "They cannot have signed with anyone yet."
B "They mustn't have signed with anyone yet."
C "They cannot signed with anyone yet."
You are certain something happened in the past: the rival lost an account. Which is correct?
A "They must lost a big account."
B "They must have lose a big account."
C "They must have lost a big account."
You want to say it is only a possibility that she missed the email. Which is best?
A "She might have missed the email."
B "She might missed the email."
C "She might have miss the email."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Read three signals from AVRT's week: a client who went quiet, a team seen at a supplier fair, and a rival that cut its price. Say what must, might, or cannot be true, using modals of deduction (must / might / could / can't + be, and + have + past participle). End with one recommendation. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 11

1They replied warmly, so they must still be interested.Eles responderam de forma calorosa, então devem continuar interessados.
2It might be a scheduling delay, or it could be internal approvals.Pode ser um atraso de agenda, ou aprovações internas.
3Our quote is open, so they cannot have signed yet.Nossa proposta está aberta, então é impossível que já tenham assinado.
4The price is below cost, so they must have lost an account.O preço está abaixo do custo, então devem ter perdido uma conta.
5Let us not jump to conclusions, but I would rule out the worst case.Vamos não tirar conclusões precipitadas, mas eu descartaria o pior cenário.
Aula 12 -- Pre-class

Who's Who at AVRT -- Describing People, Companies and Markets

Reading a company profile of AVRT and describing who is who. Key words: founder, stakeholder, subsidiary, headquarters, flagship, venture, niche, cutting-edge, proprietary, incumbent, mentor, track record. Structure: relative clauses -- defining (no commas: who/which/that/where/whose) and non-defining (with commas: who/which, never that) -- to describe and add information about people, companies and markets.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Founder -- the person who started a company (fundador / fundadora)
"The founder who started AVRT is Izabel."
Stakeholder -- anyone with an interest in a company's success (parte interessada)
"Every company has stakeholders who care about its future."
Subsidiary -- a company owned or controlled by a larger one (subsidiária)
"AVRT opened a subsidiary, which handles North American sales."
Headquarters -- the main office where a company is run (sede)
"The headquarters where AVRT operates is just outside Valinhos."
Flagship -- a company's most important product or service (carro-chefe)
"Its flagship sensor is the product that made the company's name."
Venture -- a new business project that involves risk (empreendimento)
"It began as a small venture that grew into a serious player."
Niche -- a specialized segment of the market (nicho)
"AVRT serves a niche which most large firms ignore."
Cutting-edge -- the most advanced; state of the art (de ponta / última geração)
"Clients who need cutting-edge sensors come to AVRT."
Proprietary -- owned exclusively by one company; patented (proprietário / exclusivo)
"The proprietary software that runs the sensors is AVRT's own."
Incumbent -- the established company that already dominates a market (empresa estabelecida / dominante)
"The incumbent that once dominated the segment is being caught."
Mentor -- an experienced person who advises and guides someone (mentor / mentora)
"A mentor who advised Izabel early on shaped the company."
Track record -- a history of past performance and achievements (histórico / reputação)
"A track record which clients trust is worth more than any advertisement."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Founder
Stakeholder
Subsidiary
Headquarters
Flagship
Venture
Niche
Cutting-edge
Proprietary
Incumbent
Mentor
Track record

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

AVRT, which was founded in Valinhos in 2015, is a company that designs wireless sensors. The founder who started it, Izabel, is a CEO whose background is in electronics. Its flagship product, the sensor that made the company's name, is used by factories that track equipment in real time. The engineer who leads research, and whose designs won two awards, built the team. AVRT serves a niche which large firms ignore, and the headquarters where it operates sits just outside Valinhos. The incumbent that once dominated the segment is now the company AVRT is catching.

1. "The founder who started it is Izabel." Why "who"?
A "who" introduces a relative clause about a person.
B "who" is used for things and places.
C "who" always needs commas.
2. "AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is a company..." Why the commas and "which"?
A It is a defining clause that tells us which AVRT.
B It is a non-defining clause: extra information, commas, "which" (never "that").
C Because "that" would be more formal here.
3. "a CEO whose background is in electronics." What does "whose" show?
A Possession -- the background belongs to the CEO.
B A place where something happens.
C A question about who someone is.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Relative Clauses

GRAMMAR

Describing nouns (explicação em inglês e português).

WordUse / UsoExample
whopessoas (people)The founder who started it.
which / thatcoisas (things); "that" só em definingThe sensor that we make.
wherelugares (places)The city where AVRT is based.
whoseposse (possession)A CEO whose background is in electronics.
Defining vs non-definingsem vírgula = essencial; com vírgula = informação extraAVRT, which was founded in 2015, is growing.
Cuidado / Watch outNunca use "what" como pronome relativo: errado "the company what makes..." → certo "the company that/which makes...". Na non-defining (com vírgula), use só which/who, nunca "that".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"AVRT is a company makes wireless sensors."
"The founder started it is Izabel."
"Izabel is a CEO background is in electronics."
"AVRT, was founded in 2015, is growing."
"The city AVRT is based is Valinhos."
"We serve a that most large firms ignore."

Stage 2: Put the Profile in Order

Order

Put the company profile in a logical order, from the company to the competition.

?AVRT, which was founded in Valinhos in 2015, is a company that makes sensors.
?And the incumbent that once dominated the segment is the one we are catching.
?The founder who started it is Izabel, a CEO whose background is in electronics.
?The engineer who leads research built a team that competes on quality.
?We serve a niche which most large firms ignore.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

AVRT is the company that makes wireless sensors.
AVRT é a empresa que faz sensores sem fio.
The founder who started it is Izabel.
A fundadora que a criou é a Izabel.
Izabel is a CEO whose background is in electronics.
Izabel é uma CEO cuja formação é em eletrônica.
We serve a niche which large firms ignore.
Atendemos um nicho que as grandes empresas ignoram.
AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is growing fast.
A AVRT, que foi fundada em 2015, está crescendo rápido.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You describe the company (a thing) in a defining clause. Which is correct?
A "AVRT is a company what makes sensors."
B "AVRT is a company that makes sensors."
C "AVRT is a company who makes sensors."
You add extra, non-essential information about AVRT. Which is best?
A "AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is growing."
B "AVRT, that was founded in 2015, is growing."
C "AVRT which was founded in 2015 is growing."
You describe a person (the engineer). Which is correct?
A "The engineer which leads research won two awards."
B "The engineer who leads research won two awards."
C "The engineer whose leads research won two awards."
You want to show possession (the CEO's background). Which is best?
A "Izabel is a CEO whose background is in electronics."
B "Izabel is a CEO who background is in electronics."
C "Izabel is a CEO which background is in electronics."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Introduce AVRT to a new partner in four or five sentences: the company, its flagship product, the key people, the niche, and the incumbent you are catching. Use at least four relative clauses (who / which / that / where / whose), including one non-defining clause with commas. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 12

1AVRT is the company that makes wireless sensors.A AVRT é a empresa que faz sensores sem fio.
2The founder who started it is Izabel.A fundadora que a criou é a Izabel.
3Izabel is a CEO whose background is in electronics.Izabel é uma CEO cuja formação é em eletrônica.
4We serve a niche which large firms ignore.Atendemos um nicho que as grandes empresas ignoram.
5AVRT, which was founded in 2015, is catching the incumbent.A AVRT, que foi fundada em 2015, está alcançando a líder estabelecida.
Aula 13 -- Pre-class

The Proposal -- Deciding What to Do with Verb Patterns

Weighing a business proposal — a possible merger with a small sensor startup. Key words: proposal, rationale, pitch, merger, buy-in, due diligence, valuation, upside, downside, green-light, ballpark, commitment. Structure: gerund vs infinitive (verb patterns) -- verb + to (decide to, agree to, afford to), verb + -ing (consider, suggest, avoid, recommend), it is worth + -ing, and preposition + -ing (look forward to doing).
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Proposal -- a formal plan or offer put forward for consideration (proposta)
"Andy put a clear proposal on the table: to acquire Nordic Sense."
Rationale -- the reasons and logic behind a decision (justificativa / lógica)
"The rationale for the merger is faster access to the medical market."
Pitch -- a short, persuasive presentation of an idea or offer (apresentação persuasiva)
"We decided to prepare a short pitch before meeting the founders."
Merger -- the combining of two companies into one (fusão)
"The board is keen on green-lighting a merger this year."
Buy-in -- agreement and support from the people involved (adesão / apoio)
"What matters now is getting buy-in from the other directors."
Due diligence -- careful investigation of a deal before you commit (auditoria prévia / diligência)
"We cannot afford to skip due diligence on their numbers."
Valuation -- an estimate of how much a company is worth (avaliação de valor)
"I want an independent valuation before committing to anything."
Upside -- the potential benefit or gain (potencial de ganho / vantagem)
"The upside is expanding into a new market a year faster."
Downside -- the potential risk or disadvantage (risco / desvantagem)
"The downside is the cost of merging two engineering teams."
Green-light -- to give official approval to go ahead (aprovar / dar sinal verde)
"If the numbers hold, I am happy to green-light the deal."
Ballpark -- a rough, approximate figure (estimativa aproximada)
"I would avoid naming a ballpark before the audit is finished."
Commitment -- a firm decision to do or support something (compromisso)
"A merger is a long commitment, so we should not rush it."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Proposal
Rationale
Pitch
Merger
Buy-in
Due diligence
Valuation
Upside
Downside
Green-light
Ballpark
Commitment

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Andy puts a proposal on the table. "I suggest acquiring Nordic Sense," he says. Izabel listens. "Before we decide to move," she answers, "I need the rationale." The upside is clear: the merger would let AVRT expand faster. But Izabel refuses to rush. "We cannot afford to skip due diligence, and it is worth getting an independent valuation first." Andy asks for a ballpark. She avoids naming a number and focuses on buy-in from the directors. "I propose preparing a short pitch, then meeting the founders," she says. "We look forward to hearing their side. If the numbers hold, I am happy to recommend green-lighting it."

1. "I suggest acquiring Nordic Sense." Why "acquiring" and not "to acquire"?
A Because "suggest" is followed by the -ing form (gerund).
B Because it is a question.
C Because "acquire" has no infinitive.
2. "We cannot afford to skip due diligence." Which pattern is this?
A verb + -ing.
B verb + to-infinitive (afford + to + base).
C preposition + -ing.
3. "We look forward to hearing their side." Why "hearing"?
A Here "to" is a preposition, so it takes the -ing form.
B Because "hear" is irregular.
C Because the sentence is in the past.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Verb Patterns (Gerund vs Infinitive)

GRAMMAR

Choosing between "to do" and "doing" (explicação em inglês e português).

Pattern / PadrãoCommon verbs / VerbosExample
verb + to + base
verbo + to + base		decide, agree, plan, manage, afford, hope, offer, refuse, aim, promiseWe decided to expand.
verb + -ing
verbo + gerúndio		consider, suggest, recommend, avoid, delay, postpone, risk, keep, mindWe considered opening a subsidiary.
it is worth + -ingworth, no point (in), spend timeIt is worth investing in the pitch.
preposition + -ing
preposição + gerúndio		look forward to, interested in, committed to, before / afterWe look forward to meeting them.
Cuidado / Watch outO primeiro verbo decide o padrão. Depois de decide/agree/plan/afford use "to" + base. Depois de suggest/consider/avoid e depois de "it is worth" use -ing. Em "look forward to" e "committed to" o "to" é preposição → + -ing. Errado: "decided expanding", "worth to invest", "look forward to receive".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We decided pitch the merger to the board."
"The board suggested a subsidiary first."
"It is worth proper due diligence."
"We cannot afford skip due diligence."
"We look forward to the founders next week."
"I propose a short pitch."

Stage 2: Put the Pitch in Order

Order

Put Izabel's proposal in a logical order, from the idea to the next step.

?If the numbers hold, I propose green-lighting the merger next quarter.
?The rationale is simple: we would avoid building the technology from zero.
?First, Andy suggests acquiring Nordic Sense, a small sensor startup.
?Then we plan to meet the founders and get buy-in from the directors.
?But we cannot afford to skip due diligence, so it is worth getting a valuation.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

We decided to expand into the medical market.
Decidimos expandir para o mercado médico.
The board suggested opening a subsidiary first.
O conselho sugeriu abrir uma subsidiária primeiro.
It is worth doing proper due diligence.
Vale a pena fazer uma diligência prévia adequada.
We cannot afford to rush the valuation.
Não podemos nos dar ao luxo de apressar a avaliação.
We look forward to meeting the founders.
Estamos ansiosos para conhecer os fundadores.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You want to say the company chose to grow abroad. Which is correct?
A "We decided expanding abroad."
B "We decided to expand abroad."
C "We decided expand abroad."
You think the audit is a good use of time. Which is best?
A "It is worth doing the audit first."
B "It is worth to do the audit first."
C "It is worth do the audit first."
You recommend a joint venture, not a merger. Which is correct?
A "I suggest to open a joint venture."
B "I suggest open a joint venture."
C "I suggest opening a joint venture."
You are eager to hear the founders' answer. Which is best?
A "We look forward to hearing from them."
B "We look forward to hear from them."
C "We look forward hear from them."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Imagine a proposal lands on your desk at AVRT: a merger with a small sensor startup. Weigh it out loud. Say what you decided to do, what you would consider doing, what is worth doing first, and what you look forward to. Use at least four verb patterns (decide to, suggest -ing, worth -ing, look forward to -ing). Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 13

1We decided to expand into the medical market.Decidimos expandir para o mercado médico.
2The board suggested opening a subsidiary first.O conselho sugeriu abrir uma subsidiária primeiro.
3It is worth doing proper due diligence.Vale a pena fazer uma diligência prévia adequada.
4We cannot afford to rush the valuation.Não podemos nos dar ao luxo de apressar a avaliação.
5We look forward to meeting the founders next week.Estamos ansiosos para conhecer os fundadores na próxima semana.
Aula 14 -- Pre-class

The Case for the Deal -- Making an Argument with Emphasis

Reading a written business case for the Nordic Sense deal and separating fact from opinion. Key words: compelling, merits, objection, counterargument, caveat, assertion, substantiate, bias, rebut, the bottom line, watertight, at face value. Structure: cleft sentences and emphasis -- it-cleft (It was X that ...), what-cleft (What ... is X), and "The reason ... is that ...".
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Compelling -- so convincing that it demands attention (convincente / persuasivo)
"What makes the proposal compelling is speed."
Merits -- the strong points or advantages of something (méritos / pontos fortes)
"The merits are clear, but the case must answer the objections."
Objection -- a reason someone gives for disagreeing (objeção)
"The first objection is price, and that concern is fair."
Counterargument -- a point made against another argument (contra-argumento)
"One counterargument deserves a direct rebuttal."
Caveat -- a warning or limiting condition attached to something (ressalva)
"A second caveat is integration between the two teams."
Assertion -- a confident statement presented as true (afirmação / asserção)
"That assertion rests on a bias toward size."
Substantiate -- to support a claim with solid evidence (comprovar / fundamentar)
"An independent audit will substantiate every number."
Bias -- a tendency that unfairly distorts judgment (viés)
"The claim rests on a bias toward size."
Rebut -- to argue against a point and disprove it (refutar / rebater)
"I would rebut that assertion with our track record."
The bottom line -- the single most important fact or result (o essencial / o ponto principal)
"The bottom line is that we would gain a full year."
Watertight -- so strong that it has no weakness (irrefutável / à prova de falhas)
"Read fairly, the case is close to watertight."
At face value -- accepting something exactly as it appears, without checking (pelo valor aparente / ao pé da letra)
"We should not take their figures at face value."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Compelling
Merits
Objection
Counterargument
Caveat
Assertion
Substantiate
Bias
Rebut
The bottom line
Watertight
At face value

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Izabel writes the memo. "What makes the case compelling is speed," she begins. "It is their proprietary technology that puts us in the medical market a year sooner." She does not hide the objections. "The main one is price," she admits, "so we will substantiate every number and not take their figures at face value." When a director assumes a rival will copy the design, she rebuts it: "What the record shows is the opposite. It was reliability, not scale, that won our clients." She ends with the recommendation: "The reason we back the deal is that the upside is real and the downside is manageable."

1. "It is their proprietary technology that puts us in the market sooner." What is being emphasized?
A Their proprietary technology (the it-cleft puts it in the spotlight).
B The medical market.
C The word "sooner".
2. "What the record shows is the opposite." Why "What ... is"?
A It is a real question.
B It is a what-cleft: it emphasizes what comes after "is".
C Because "record" is plural.
3. "The reason we back the deal is that the upside is real." Which word is correct after "reason"?
A "is that" (the reason ... is that ...).
B "is because" (the reason ... is because ...).
C "is why".

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Cleft Sentences & Emphasis

GRAMMAR

Putting one idea in the spotlight (explicação em inglês e português).

Structure / EstruturaForm / FormaExample
It-cleft
ênfase com "It"		It + be + X + that / whoIt was the lead time that won it.
What-cleft
ênfase com "What"		What + clause + be + XWhat won it was the lead time.
The reason ... is thatThe reason + clause + is thatThe reason we back it is that it saves a year.
Cuidado / Watch outComece a it-cleft com It (errado: "Was the lead time that..."). Na what-cleft não ponha um it extra antes de "was" (errado: "What convinced them it was..."). Depois de "The reason ..." use is that, nunca "is because".

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

" was the lead time that won the deal."
" convinced the board was the track record."
"It was reliability won our clients."
"The we recommend it is that the upside is real."
"The reason it works is it answers every objection."
"What the record shows that reliability wins."

Stage 2: Put the Case in Order

Order

Put the written case in a logical order, from the strongest point to the recommendation.

?The reason we recommend the deal is that the upside is real and the risk is manageable.
?It is their proprietary technology that puts us in the market a year sooner.
?What makes the case compelling is speed.
?One caveat remains: what could slow both teams is a clash of engineering cultures.
?The main objection is price, so an audit will substantiate every number.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

It was the lead time that won the deal.
Foi o prazo de entrega que ganhou o negócio.
What convinced the board was the track record.
O que convenceu o conselho foi o histórico.
The bottom line is that we would gain a year.
O essencial é que ganharíamos um ano.
The reason we recommend it is that the upside is real.
A razão pela qual recomendamos é que o potencial de ganho é real.
We should not take their figures at face value.
Não deveríamos aceitar os números deles pelo valor aparente.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You want to emphasize that the lead time was the deciding factor. Which is correct?
A "Was the lead time that won the deal."
B "It was the lead time that won the deal."
C "The lead time it won the deal."
You want to emphasize what the board wanted. Which is best?
A "What the board wanted was hard evidence."
B "What the board wanted it was hard evidence."
C "What the board wanted hard evidence."
You want to give the reason for the recommendation. Which is correct?
A "The reason we recommend it is because the upside is real."
B "The reason we recommend it is that the upside is real."
C "The reason we recommend it that the upside is real."
A director says a rival will copy the technology. How do you rebut it with emphasis?
A "What the record shows is the opposite: it was reliability that won our clients."
B "The record shows the opposite reliability won our clients."
C "Was reliability that won our clients, is the record."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Make the case for one real decision at AVRT (or a decision you would defend at work). Emphasize the single most important point, answer one likely objection, and give your recommendation. Use at least three emphatic structures: "It was ... that ...", "What ... is ...", and "The reason ... is that ...". Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 14

1What makes the case compelling is speed.O que torna o argumento convincente é a velocidade.
2It was the lead time that won the deal.Foi o prazo de entrega que ganhou o negócio.
3The bottom line is that we would gain a year.O essencial é que ganharíamos um ano.
4What the record shows is that reliability wins.O que o histórico mostra é que a confiabilidade vence.
5The reason we recommend it is that the upside is real.A razão pela qual recomendamos é que o potencial de ganho é real.
Lesson 15 -- Pre-class

The Post-Mortem -- What We Would Have Done Differently

Running a project review of a launch that fell short of target — the Halo sensor line. Key words: hindsight, setback, root cause, overlook, misjudge, underestimate, backfire, turning point, salvage, write off, shortfall, momentum. Structure: third conditional (If + had + past participle, would have + past participle) and the mixed conditional (past cause, present result: "If we had hired her, we would not be firefighting now").
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Hindsight -- understanding of an event only after it has happened
"In hindsight, the shortfall was avoidable."
Setback -- a problem that delays or reverses progress
"The firmware defect was the setback that hurt the most."
Root cause -- the fundamental reason a problem happens
"The root cause was speed: we rushed the decision."
Overlook -- to fail to notice or consider something
"We overlooked how fast the competition would cut prices."
Misjudge -- to form a wrong opinion or estimate of something
"We misjudged the market because we were in a hurry."
Underestimate -- to think something is smaller or weaker than it really is
"We underestimated the competition."
Backfire -- to produce the opposite result to the one intended
"If we do not learn from this, the next launch will backfire."
Turning point -- the moment when a situation changes decisively
"This review is the turning point for the next launch."
Salvage -- to rescue something of value from a failure
"There is real momentum, so we can salvage the line."
Write off -- to accept that something is a total loss
"I am not writing the product off; it can be saved."
Shortfall -- the amount by which something falls short of the target
"The headline is a thirty percent shortfall against target."
Momentum -- the force that keeps progress moving forward
"If we had not built the brand, there would be no momentum now."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Hindsight
Setback
Root cause
Overlook
Misjudge
Underestimate
Backfire
Turning point
Salvage
Write off
Shortfall
Momentum

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Izabel opens the post-mortem on the Halo launch, which missed its target. "In hindsight, the shortfall was avoidable," she says. "If we had run a regional pilot, we would have caught the pricing problem. And if we had tested with real customers, we would have found the firmware defect in a week." The root cause was speed: they misjudged the market and underestimated the competition. Andy asks whether they should write the line off. "No," she answers. "There is real momentum. If we had not built the brand so carefully, there would be nothing to salvage now — but there is. And if we had hired an operations lead a year earlier, we would not be firefighting today."

1. "If we had run a pilot, we would have caught it." Did AVRT run a pilot?
A No — the third conditional imagines a past that did not happen.
B Yes, and it worked well.
C Yes, but only in one region.
2. "If we had hired her earlier, we would not be firefighting now." Which conditional is this?
A A pure third conditional (past result).
B A mixed conditional — past cause, present result.
C A second conditional.
3. Why "had run" and not "would have run" in the if-clause?
A The if-clause uses had + past participle; "would" never goes in the if-clause.
B Because "run" is irregular.
C Because the sentence is a question.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- Third & Mixed Conditionals

GRAMMAR

Talking about an imagined past and its results.

TypeStructureExample
Third conditional
imagined past		If + had + past participle, ... would/could/might have + past participleIf we had tested it, we would have found the bug.
Mixed conditional
past cause, present result		If + had + past participle, ... would + base (now)If we had hired her, we would not be firefighting now.
OrderBoth orders work. If-clause first → use a comma. Result first → no comma: "We would have caught it if we had run a pilot."
Watch outNEVER use "would" in the if-clause — use had + past participle. NEVER drop "have" in the result. Wrong: "If we would have tested", "we would caught it", "we would not have been firefighting now" (when the result is happening now).

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"If we the firmware, we would have found the defect."
"If the forecast had been realistic, we fewer components."
"We would have shipped on schedule if the supplier the delay."
"If we had run a pilot, we the pricing problem."
"If we had hired her last year, we firefighting now."
"If we had acted sooner, we the launch."

Stage 2: Put the Post-Mortem in Order

Order

Put Izabel's review in a logical order, from the result to the fix.

?If we had tested with real customers, we would have found the defect in a week.
?The Halo launch missed target by thirty percent — a serious shortfall.
?If we had run a regional pilot, we would have caught the pricing problem.
?In hindsight, the root cause was speed: we rushed and misjudged the market.
?There is still momentum, so we will fix the process and salvage the line.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

If we had run a pilot, we would have caught the pricing problem.
If we had tested with customers, we would have found the defect.
The root cause was speed: we misjudged the market.
If we had hired her earlier, we would not be firefighting now.
There is still momentum, so we can salvage the line.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You want to say a test would have prevented a bug. Which is correct?
A "If we would have tested it, we would have caught the bug."
B "If we had tested it, we would have caught the bug."
C "If we tested it, we would have caught the bug."
The result you mean is happening NOW. Which sentence fits?
A "If we had hired her, we would not be firefighting now."
B "If we had hired her, we would not have been firefighting now."
C "If we hire her, we will not be firefighting now."
You want to say a faster response could have saved the launch. Which is best?
A "If we acted sooner, we could salvage the launch."
B "If we had acted sooner, we could have salvaged the launch."
C "If we had acted sooner, we could salvaged the launch."
You want to name why the project failed. Which is most precise?
A "The root cause was speed; we underestimated the competition."
B "The root cause was speed; we underestimate the competition."
C "The root cause was speed; we would underestimate the competition."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of a real project at AVRT that did not go as planned. Run a short post-mortem out loud: name the shortfall and the root cause, then say what you would have done differently and what you can still salvage. Use at least four third conditionals and one mixed conditional. Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 15

1If we had run a pilot, we would have caught the pricing problem.
2If we had tested with customers, we would have found the defect.
3The root cause was speed: we misjudged the market.
4If we had hired her earlier, we would not be firefighting now.
5There is still momentum, so we can salvage the line.
Lesson 16 -- Pre-class

The Recovery Plan -- Delegating the Fixes

Reading the Halo recovery plan and seeing how each fix is delegated to an owner. Key words: turnaround, overhaul, rectify, escalate, accountable, remediation, sign off on, third party, rework, handover, take ownership, get up to speed. Structure: the causative -- have / get something done, have someone do, and get someone to do it.
0%
▼

Stage 1.1: Vocabulary Cards

Vocabulary

Listen to each word and read the example. Tap Listen to hear it.

Turnaround -- a recovery that reverses a failing situation
"This plan turns a setback into a turnaround."
Overhaul -- a thorough repair or redesign of something
"We had the release process given a full overhaul."
Rectify -- to correct a mistake or a fault
"Where we cannot rectify a gap in-house, we bring in help."
Escalate -- to pass a problem to a higher level for action
"Any missed date is escalated to me the same day."
Accountable -- required to explain results and answer for them
"Each item has one accountable owner."
Remediation -- the work of fixing a defect or problem
"Remediation is not open-ended: every task has a date."
Sign off on -- to give something final, formal approval
"Nobody signs off on a shipment until quality certifies it."
Third party -- an outside company or person, not you or the client
"We bring in a third party rather than let it drift."
Rework -- to do a piece of work again to correct it
"We had the contract reworked so delays are flagged."
Handover -- the passing of a task or responsibility to someone else
"Each workstream has a clear handover."
Take ownership -- to accept full responsibility for something
"In the past, nobody took ownership of a fault."
Get up to speed -- to reach the level of knowledge a task needs
"We are getting the whole team up to speed on the process."

Stage 1.2: Matching

Practice

Match each word or expression with the correct definition.

Turnaround
Overhaul
Rectify
Escalate
Accountable
Remediation
Sign off on
Third party
Rework
Handover
Take ownership
Get up to speed

Stage 1.3: Grammar in Context

GRAMMAR

Read the text and answer the questions.

Izabel opens the recovery plan. "We will not fix everything ourselves," she writes. "We are having the firmware rewritten by an external security firm, and we will get the whole system retested before any customer sees it." On the forecast, she is clear: "We are getting an independent analyst to rebuild the model." She has already had the release checklist overhauled. The rule is simple: nobody signs off on a shipment until quality has certified it, and every item has one accountable owner who must get the fix done by Thursday.

1. "We are having the firmware rewritten by an external firm." Who writes the code?
A Izabel and her in-house team.
B The external security firm (Izabel arranges it, someone else does it).
C Nobody — it is already finished.
2. "We are getting an analyst to rebuild the model." Which word is required after "analyst"?
A "to" (get + person + to + base verb).
B nothing (get + person + base verb).
C "that".
3. "She has had the release checklist overhauled." What is the correct word order?
A have + object + past participle (had the checklist overhauled).
B have + past participle + object (had overhauled the checklist).
C Both are the same.

Stage 1.4: Grammar Tip -- The Causative

GRAMMAR

Delegating the work: you arrange it, someone else does it.

PatternFormExample
Result (the thing)have / get + object + past participleWe had the firmware rewritten.
Doer with "have"have + person + base verbWe had the vendor flag delays.
Doer with "get"get + person + to + base verbWe got an analyst to rebuild it.
Watch outKeep the order have + object + past participle. "We had the firmware rewritten" (someone else did it) is not "We had rewritten the firmware" (present perfect, you did it). Use "to" after get + person, but no "to" after have + person.

Stage 1.5: Fill in the Blank

Practice

Complete each sentence. Tap Listen to hear the full sentence.

"We had the firmware by a firm."
"We got an analyst rebuild the model."
"We had the vendor every delay."
"We are the contract reworked."
"Each owner must get the fix by Thursday."
"Nobody off on a shipment until quality certifies it."

Stage 2: Put the Plan in Order

Order

Put the recovery plan in a logical order, from the first fix to accountability.

?Every item has one accountable owner who must get the fix done by Thursday.
?We are having the firmware rewritten by an external security firm.
?This plan sets out how we will rescue the Halo line.
?Nobody signs off on a shipment until quality has certified it.
?We are getting an independent analyst to rebuild the forecast model.

Stage 3: Pronunciation

Speaking

Listen to each sentence, then record yourself saying it.

We are having the firmware rewritten by a specialist.
We got an independent analyst to rebuild the forecast.
We had the vendor flag every delay in writing.
Nobody signs off on a shipment until quality certifies it.
Each owner must get the fix done by Thursday.

Stage 4: Situational Quiz

Quiz

Choose the best answer for each executive situation.

You want to say a security firm is rewriting the firmware for you. Which is correct?
A "We had rewritten the firmware by a security firm."
B "We are having the firmware rewritten by a security firm."
C "We are having rewritten the firmware by a security firm."
You persuaded an analyst to rebuild the model. Which is best?
A "We got an analyst to rebuild the model."
B "We got an analyst rebuild the model."
C "We got rebuild the model an analyst."
You asked the vendor to flag every delay. Using "have someone", which is correct?
A "We had the vendor to flag every delay."
B "We had the vendor flag every delay."
C "We had flagged the vendor every delay."
A director asks who is responsible for a fix. How do you answer?
A "Each item has one accountable owner who must get the fix done."
B "The fix is get done by someone."
C "We had done the fix by an owner accountable."

Stage 5: Free Production

Reflection

Record yourself answering the question below. There is no right or wrong answer.

Think of one real problem at AVRT (or at your work) that needs fixing. Explain your recovery plan: name the fix, say who you would have do it, and who owns it. Use at least three causative structures: "have something done", "get someone to do it", and "have someone do it". Take your time.

Survival Card -- Lesson 16

1We are having the firmware rewritten by a specialist.
2We got an independent analyst to rebuild the forecast.
3Nobody signs off on a shipment until quality certifies it.
4We bring in a third party rather than let it drift.
5Each owner must get the fix done by Thursday.

IN CLASS -- Selecione a Aula

01
Mapping Your Executive Voice
Introducing yourself and your role as CEO of AVRT -- 31 slides
02
The CEO Introducing Herself on a Thursday Call
Present simple, frequency adverbs, and how the weekly call runs -- 28 slides
03
What Happened This Week
Reporting past actions in the Thursday meeting -- simple past, regular and irregular -- 31 slides
04
The Story So Far
Talking about progress and results with the present perfect -- 31 slides
05
What Do You Recommend?
Making recommendations in the Thursday meeting -- modals of advice: should, ought to, had better, I would recommend -- 31 slides
06
How AVRT Works
Describing processes and reports with the passive voice -- is/are + past participle -- 31 slides
07
The Road Ahead
Talking about plans and the next quarter -- will vs going to vs present continuous -- 31 slides
08
The Market Outlook
Reading a market outlook and planning for each scenario -- the first conditional (if + present, will + base) -- 31 slides
09
The What-If Room
Weighing hypothetical strategy and negotiation scenarios -- the second conditional (if + past simple, would + base) -- 31 slides
10
The Meeting, Relayed
Reading the minutes of a board meeting and relaying what was said -- reported speech (backshift, say vs tell, reported questions and commands) -- 31 slides
11
Reading the Signals
Interpreting an ambiguous business situation -- modals of deduction (must / might / could / can't + be and + have + participle) -- 31 slides
12
Who's Who at AVRT
Reading a company profile and describing people, companies and markets -- relative clauses (defining and non-defining: who, which, that, where, whose) -- 31 slides
13
The Proposal
Weighing a business proposal and deciding what to do -- gerund vs infinitive (verb patterns: decide to, suggest -ing, worth -ing, look forward to -ing) -- 31 slides
14
The Case for the Deal
Reading a written business case and separating fact from opinion -- cleft sentences and emphasis (It was ... that ...; What ... is ...; The reason ... is that ...) -- 31 slides
15
The Post-Mortem
Running a project review of a launch that fell short -- third and mixed conditionals (If we had tested it, we would have caught it; If we had hired her, we would not be firefighting now) -- 31 slides
16
The Recovery Plan
Reading a recovery plan that delegates the fixes after the failed launch -- the causative (have/get something done; have someone do; get someone to do) -- 31 slides

Atividades Complementares

Materiais extras para reforçar o aprendizado fora de aula. Marque como concluído após assistir/ouvir.

Lesson 1 — Mapping Your Executive Voice

Series
Industry (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Induction"

Young professionals introduce themselves, state their roles, and describe their responsibilities in a high-pressure finance world. Notice how they say what they are in charge of, who they report to, and what they deal with on a daily basis -- exactly your Thursday-call vocabulary.

Tip: watch with English subtitles. Note 3 expressions the characters use to describe their roles and responsibilities.

Watch on HBO →
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Introducing Yourself and Your Role"

Clear, executive-level episodes on how to describe your position, responsibilities, and reporting line in real meetings. Direct connection to Lesson 1: the natural phrases native speakers use to introduce themselves and ask questions back.

Tip: listen twice. Write down 5 phrases you could use to introduce yourself on the Thursday call.

Listen at Business English Pod →
YouTube
"How to Introduce Yourself Professionally in a Meeting" -- Speak Confident English

A practical lesson on executive self-introductions: role, responsibilities, reporting line, and the questions you ask back -- with ready-to-use present simple patterns. Connection to Lesson 1: exactly the structures you will use with your American partners.

Tip: pause and repeat each introduction aloud. Record your own version 3 times without freezing.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 2 — The CEO Introducing Herself on a Thursday Call

Series
Succession (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 2: "Sh*t Show at the F**k Factory"

Executives open and run high-stakes meetings: they give updates, oversee operations, and buy time when they don't have an answer. Notice the present simple for roles and the phrases they use to open the room -- exactly your Thursday call, at a bigger scale.

Tip: watch one meeting scene with English subtitles. Note how each person opens and how they handle a hard question.

Watch on HBO →
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Running Effective Meetings: Opening and Chairing"

Executive-level episodes on opening a meeting, giving updates, and keeping control of the room. Direct connection to Lesson 2: the exact phrases to open the Thursday call and to buy time politely when you need to check something.

Tip: listen twice. Write down 5 phrases for opening and chairing your weekly call.

Listen at Business English Pod →
YouTube
"English for Meetings: How to Chair and Run a Meeting" -- Speak Confident English

A practical lesson on frequency adverbs and the present simple for describing routines, plus the language to open and run a meeting. Connection to Lesson 2: ready-to-use openers and the "let me check that and get back to you" strategy.

Tip: pause and repeat each opener aloud. Record your own 90-second Thursday-call opening.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 3 — What Happened This Week

Series
Industry (HBO) -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Induction"

Young finance professionals report results and defend what they did the day before to demanding managers. Listen for the constant simple past: "I closed it," "the trade settled," "we didn't hit the number." It is exactly your Thursday recap, in a high-pressure trading floor.

Tip: watch one review scene with English subtitles. Write down five past-tense verbs (regular and irregular) people use to report results.

Watch on HBO →
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Reporting Progress and Giving Project Updates"

Executive-level episodes on giving status updates and recapping what happened. Direct connection to Lesson 3: the exact past-tense phrases to report what you delivered, closed, and resolved, and how to explain what you did not finish.

Tip: listen twice. Write down five sentences with "did not / didn't + base verb" that you could use in your own recap.

Listen at Business English Pod →
YouTube
"Past Simple: Regular and Irregular Verbs" -- Speak Confident English

A clear lesson on the simple past: -ed endings, common irregular verbs, and forming negatives and questions with "did". Connection to Lesson 3: the pronunciation of the three -ed sounds and the "did not + base verb" rule you practiced in class.

Tip: pause after each irregular verb and say it aloud. Then record your own 90-second recap of last week at AVRT.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 4 — The Story So Far

Series
The Founder (2016 film) -- Ray Kroc scales up McDonald's

A company grows from one shop to a giant. The whole film is a "story so far": how far the business has come, what it has reached, and what it has not solved yet. Listen for the language of growth, benchmarks, and progress over time.

Tip: watch one scene with English subtitles. Write five present-perfect sentences about how the business has changed.

See on IMDb →
Podcast
Business English Pod -- "Giving a Company Update and Reporting Results"

Executive episodes on summarizing progress and results to a board or team. Direct connection to Lesson 4: the present-perfect language to say what your company has achieved so far and what it has not finished yet.

Tip: listen twice. Note five phrases that mix "since / for / so far / yet" with the present perfect.

Listen at Business English Pod →
YouTube
"Present Perfect vs Past Simple" -- Speak Confident English

A clear lesson on when to use the present perfect (change up to now) versus the simple past (finished time), with "since", "for", "just", "yet", and "already". Direct connection to Lesson 4: exactly the choice you practiced when reporting AVRT's progress.

Tip: pause after each example and repeat it aloud. Then record a 90-second "story so far" about your year.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 5 — What Do You Recommend?

Series
Suits -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Lawyers at a New York firm advise clients and each other all day long. Listen for constant recommendations: "You should take the deal," "I would advise against that," "We had better move fast." It is exactly the language you use when the board asks for your view.

Tip: watch one advising scene with English subtitles. Write down five sentences with should, ought to, had better, or "I would recommend / advise".

Series overview & where to watch →
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast (Harvard Business Review)

A weekly podcast where leading business thinkers give recommendations to executives. Direct connection to Lesson 5: hear how advisors phrase strong and soft advice -- "I would recommend...", "You should prioritize...", "Companies had better..." -- in real leadership conversations.

Tip: pick one episode on strategy. Write down five recommendations the guest makes, then rewrite them as your own advice for AVRT.

Listen at HBR →
YouTube
"How to Use Should, Ought to, Supposed to and Had Better" -- Oxford Online English

A clear lesson on the modal verbs of advice: should, ought to, and had better -- when each one is stronger or softer, and why there is no "to" after should or had better. Exactly the code you practiced in Lesson 5.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about AVRT. Then record a 90-second set of recommendations for the board.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 6 — How AVRT Works

Series
How It's Made (Science Channel) -- any episode

A documentary that shows how everyday products are manufactured, from raw material to finished item. The narration is almost entirely passive voice: "The metal is cut, the parts are welded, and each unit is inspected." It is the exact language you use to describe the AVRT process.

Tip: watch one five-minute segment and write down ten passive sentences ("It is heated", "They are packed"). Then describe one AVRT step the same way.

Series overview & where to watch →
Podcast
Grammar Girl -- Quick and Dirty Tips (passive voice episodes)

Short, clear episodes on English grammar and style, including when to use the passive voice and when to avoid it. Direct connection to Lesson 6: it explains why "The report is submitted" often sounds more natural in business than naming the doer.

Tip: search "passive voice" on the site. Note two cases where the passive is the better choice, and two where the active is clearer.

Listen at Quick and Dirty Tips →
YouTube
"Present and past passives -- 6 Minute Grammar" -- BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on the passive in the present and past: be + past participle, and how only the "be" changes to show the tense. Exactly the code you practiced in Lesson 6.

Tip: pause after each example and make your own AVRT version. Then record a 90-second description of how a product is manufactured and shipped.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 7 — The Road Ahead

Series
Billions -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Pilot"

Hedge-fund and corporate leaders spend every scene talking about the road ahead: the next move, the forecast, the plan. Listen for future forms in action -- "We are going to build this," "I will handle it," "We are meeting them Thursday." It is exactly the language you use when the board asks about your roadmap.

Tip: watch one strategy scene with English subtitles. Write down five future sentences and mark whether each one is "will", "going to", or present continuous.

Series overview & where to watch →
Podcast
How I Built This with Guy Raz (NPR)

Founders tell the story of how they built their companies -- and, above all, how they planned the road ahead. Direct connection to Lesson 7: hear real leaders describe roadmaps, forecasts, and the plans they were going to make happen, in natural future forms.

Tip: pick one episode about a company you admire. Write down five sentences where the founder talks about the future, then rewrite them as your own plan for AVRT.

Listen at NPR →
YouTube
"Will, going to, present continuous -- 3 English future forms" -- Simple English Videos

A clear lesson on the three ways English talks about the future: "will" for instant decisions and predictions, "be going to" for decided plans, and the present continuous for fixed arrangements. Exactly the code you practiced in Lesson 7 -- and why "I meet them Tuesday" should be "I am meeting them on Tuesday".

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about AVRT. Then record a 90-second roadmap for the next quarter using all three forms.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 8 — The Market Outlook

Film
The Big Short (2015)

A sharp, funny film about a volatile market and the people who read the outlook before everyone else. Listen for scenario language everywhere: "If the housing market falls, this will happen," "Unless something changes, the whole thing collapses." It is the first conditional applied to real markets, risk, and downturns.

Tip: watch one scene where an analyst explains a scenario. Write down five sentences with "if", "unless", or "as long as", and mark the if-part (present) and the result (will).

Film overview & where to watch →
Podcast
Planet Money (NPR)

A short, clear economics podcast about demand, competition, downturns, and the scenarios that shape a market. Direct connection to Lesson 8: hear real analysts say what will happen if demand surges, if a competitor moves in, or if the economy slows -- the first conditional in its natural home.

Tip: pick one episode about a market or a company. Write down five "if / unless" predictions the hosts make, then rewrite them as scenarios for AVRT.

Listen at NPR →
YouTube
"The First Conditional -- Conditionals with Georgie" -- BBC Learning English

A clear lesson on the first conditional: if + present simple for a real future condition, and will + base for the result. Exactly the code you practiced in Lesson 8 -- and why "if demand will rise" should be "if demand rises".

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about AVRT. Then record a 90-second market outlook using "if", "unless", and "as long as".

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 9 — The What-If Room

Series
Succession -- Season 1, Episode 1: "Celebration"

The Roy family fights for control of a media empire, and every scene is a what-if: "If we sold now, we would lose control," "If I were you, I would not sign." It is the second conditional in a boardroom — leverage, concessions, walking away, and weighing every trade-off out loud, exactly like the what-if room in Lesson 9.

Tip: watch one negotiation scene with English subtitles. Write down three hypothetical sentences ("If they offered X, I would...") and check that the if-clause is past simple and the result uses "would".

Series overview & where to watch →
Podcast
Negotiate Anything with Kwame Christian

The leading negotiation podcast, with over 1,600 episodes on how to hold difficult conversations, make concessions, use leverage, and know when to walk away. Direct connection to Lesson 9: hear real negotiators reason in hypotheticals — "if they did X, I would..." — the natural home of the second conditional.

Tip: pick one episode about closing a deal. Note three "what would you do if..." moments, then answer each one yourself as a full second conditional about AVRT.

Listen on Apple Podcasts →
YouTube
"The Second Conditional -- 6 Minute Grammar" -- BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on the second conditional: if + past simple, would + base verb, for unreal and hypothetical situations. Exactly the code you practiced in Lesson 9 — including why "If we would have..." is wrong and "If I were you..." is right.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about AVRT. Then record a 90-second what-if brief using at least four second-conditional sentences.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 10 — The Meeting, Relayed

Film
Spotlight (2015)

A gripping true story about Boston Globe journalists who relay and report what sources said. The whole film is reported speech in action: "She told us that...", "He said the records had been sealed", "They asked whether we could prove it." A masterclass in how professionals report what people said, accurately and on the record.

Tip: pick one interview scene. Write down five things a source says in direct speech, then rewrite each as reported speech with correct backshift (said that... had..., told... would...).

Film overview & where to watch →
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast (Harvard Business Review)

A weekly podcast where hosts and guests relay meetings, decisions, and research from the business world. Listen for the natural reporting language executives use: "The CEO explained that...", "She told the board that revenue had grown", "They asked whether the plan would work." Exactly the register you need to relay your own board meetings.

Tip: pick one episode about leadership or strategy. Note five sentences where the host reports what a guest said, and mark the reporting verb (said, told, explained, admitted) and the backshift.

Listen at HBR →
YouTube
"Can you use REPORTED SPEECH? Grammar Lesson + Examples" -- mmmEnglish

A clear, practical lesson on reported speech: the backshift (present -> past, will -> would, can -> could), say vs tell, and how to report questions and commands. Exactly the code you practiced in Lesson 10 -- and why "she said me" should be "she told me", and "asked can we" should be "asked whether we could".

Tip: pause after each example and relay it about your own meeting. Then record a 90-second recap of a real meeting using said / told / explained, one reported question, and one reported command.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 11 — Reading the Signals

Series
Sherlock -- Season 1, Episode 1: "A Study in Pink"

Sherlock Holmes reads a room the way Lesson 11 asks you to read a business signal. Every scene is deduction out loud: "You must have served abroad," "This can't have been an accident," "She might have hidden it here." It is the modal-of-deduction engine in action — must, might, could, can't — turning small clues into confident conclusions.

Tip: watch one deduction scene with English subtitles. Write down three of Sherlock's inferences and check the form: modal + be, or modal + have + past participle. Then rewrite one about a signal at AVRT.

Series overview & where to watch →
Podcast
Hidden Brain with Shankar Vedantam

A podcast about the unconscious patterns behind how we judge situations — why we jump to conclusions, trust a gut feeling, or read too much into a silence. Direct connection to Lesson 11: hear real cases of people inferring, speculating, and ruling things out, and notice how careful reasoning avoids the false certainty of a bad assumption.

Tip: pick one episode about judgment or decisions. Note three moments where someone reads a signal, then restate each as a deduction about AVRT: "They must have...", "It might be...", "They can't have...".

Listen at hiddenbrain.org →
YouTube
"How to use modals of deduction and speculation -- 6 Minute Grammar" -- BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on exactly the code from Lesson 11: must, might, could and can't to say how sure you are, both about now (must be) and about the past (must have + past participle). It covers the classic traps, including why "can't have" is right for an impossible past.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about a signal at AVRT. Then record a 90-second read-out of the week using at least four modals of deduction.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 12 — Who's Who at AVRT

Film
The Founder (2016)

The story of Ray Kroc, the salesman who turned McDonald's into a global company, and the two brothers whose restaurant started it all. It is a case study in the exact vocabulary of Lesson 12: the founder who built it, the flagship product that made its name, the venture that grew, and the incumbent that everyone had to catch. Every character is a person whose choices you can describe with a relative clause.

Tip: watch one scene with English subtitles. Write three sentences about the people and company using relative clauses ("the man who...", "the company that...", "the brothers whose..."). Check who vs which vs that.

Film overview & where to watch →
Podcast
Acquired -- The Company Histories Podcast

Deep dives into how the world's greatest companies were built. Each episode is one long, spoken company profile: the founders who started it, the flagship product that defined it, the niche it owned, and the incumbents it faced. It is exactly the register of Lesson 12 -- describing people, companies and markets with relative clauses, at length and out loud.

Tip: pick one company you admire. Note three sentences the hosts use to describe it, then write your own AVRT profile with at least two defining and one non-defining relative clause.

Listen at acquired.fm →
YouTube
"Using defining and non-defining relative clauses" -- BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on exactly the code from Lesson 12: who, which, that, where and whose, and the difference between defining clauses (no commas, essential) and non-defining clauses (commas, extra information). It covers the classic traps, including why you never use "that" after a comma.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about AVRT. Then record a 90-second company introduction using at least four relative clauses, one of them non-defining.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 13 — The Proposal

Film
Moneyball (2011)

Billy Beane puts an unconventional proposal on the table: rebuild the team with data, not instinct. The whole film is the language of Lesson 13 — deciding to change, refusing to follow the old way, considering trades, avoiding the safe option, and choosing to commit to a plan. It is a masterclass in weighing a bold proposal, with a clear rationale, upside and downside.

Tip: watch the scene where Beane pitches his idea. Write down five verb patterns you hear or could use ("he decided to...", "they suggested trading...", "it is worth taking the risk"). Then rewrite one about a decision at AVRT.

Film overview & where to watch →
Podcast
The Pitch

The only podcast where real founders pitch real venture capitalists, who decide on air whether to invest. Direct connection to Lesson 13: you hear a proposal, its rationale, its upside and downside, and the investors deciding to commit, agreeing to explore, or avoiding the deal — all in natural, unscripted English full of verb patterns.

Tip: pick one episode. Note three moments where someone weighs the deal, then restate each as a decision about AVRT: "We decided to...", "It is worth...", "We look forward to...".

Listen at thepitch.show →
YouTube
"Verb patterns -- 6 Minute Grammar" -- BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on exactly the code from Lesson 13: when a verb is followed by the -ing form and when it takes "to" + base, and what to do when a verb can take either. It walks through the common groups — decide, agree and plan with "to"; suggest, avoid and consider with -ing.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about a decision at AVRT. Then record a 90-second pitch of a proposal using at least four verb patterns.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 14 — The Case for the Deal

Film
Moneyball (2011)

Billy Beane builds a winning baseball team on a tiny budget by trusting the data instead of the scouts' opinions. It is Lesson 14 in a single film: a compelling case built on evidence, a room full of objections and bias, and a leader who has to substantiate every claim and rebut the assertion that "this is how it has always been done." Watch how the strongest argument is the one that answers its own counterarguments.

Tip: pick one scene where Beane defends his plan. Write three emphatic sentences about it -- "It was ... that ...", "What ... is ...", "The reason ... is that ..." -- separating the facts from the opinions.

Film overview & where to watch →
Podcast
HBR IdeaCast -- Harvard Business Review

A weekly conversation with the leading thinkers in business and management. Many episodes are, in effect, a spoken business case: a guest states the bottom line, anticipates the objections, substantiates the merits with evidence, and rebuts the common bias. It is the exact register of Lesson 14 -- building and defending an argument out loud, with emphasis, for a sceptical audience.

Tip: choose one episode on a decision or strategy. Note two sentences where the guest emphasizes a key point, then record your own 90-second case for a real AVRT decision using at least two cleft structures.

Listen at hbr.org →
YouTube
"CLEFT sentences -- how to use EMPHASIS in English" -- Mónica Redondo Arias

A clear walkthrough of exactly the code from Lesson 14: it-clefts ("It was X that ..."), what-clefts ("What ... is X"), and how emphasis moves one idea into the spotlight. It covers the traps you practised -- starting the it-cleft with "It", avoiding the extra "it" in a what-cleft, and using "the reason ... is that", never "is because".

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about AVRT out loud. Then write four emphatic sentences defending one real business decision -- at least one it-cleft, one what-cleft, and one "The reason ... is that ...".

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 15 — The Post-Mortem

Film
Margin Call (2011)

Over one night, a Wall Street firm runs a live post-mortem on a risk it overlooked: what was the root cause, what was the shortfall, and what they would have done if they had seen it sooner. The whole film is the language of Lesson 15 — hindsight, a decisive turning point, and hard choices about what to write off and what to salvage.

Tip: after the "if only we had acted sooner" moments, write down five third conditionals you hear or could say ("If they had flagged the risk, they would have..."). Then rewrite one about the Halo launch at AVRT.

Film overview & where to watch →
Podcast
How I Built This (NPR) -- with Guy Raz

In almost every episode, a founder looks back at a setback and says what they would have done differently — the exact hindsight of Lesson 15. You hear real post-mortems on launches that fell short, the root causes, and how the founders salvaged momentum instead of writing the business off, all in natural, unscripted English.

Tip: pick one episode and note three moments of hindsight. Restate each as a third conditional about AVRT: "If we had..., we would have...". Then record a 90-second post-mortem of a real decision.

Show overview & where to listen →
YouTube
"How to use the third conditional -- 6 Minute Grammar" -- BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on exactly the code from Lesson 15: how the third conditional imagines a past that did not happen (If + had + past participle, would have + past participle), the meaning of regret and hindsight, and the mistakes to avoid — especially putting "would" in the if-clause.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about the Halo launch. Then record a 90-second post-mortem using at least four third conditionals and one mixed conditional.

Watch on YouTube →

Lesson 16 — The Recovery Plan

Film
Apollo 13 (1995)

A launch goes wrong, and Mission Control runs the ultimate recovery plan. It is Lesson 16 in a single film: a leader who does not fix everything himself, but delegates. He has the crew shut down systems, gets the engineers to build a filter from spare parts, and holds one owner accountable for each fix. Watch how the turnaround happens by getting the right work done by the right people, on a deadline.

Tip: pick one scene where a problem is delegated. Write three causative sentences about it -- "They had the filter built", "They got the crew to power down", "They had the engineers test it".

Film overview & where to watch →
Podcast
WorkLife with Adam Grant -- TED

An organizational psychologist takes you inside how great teams work. Several episodes are, in effect, a recovery plan out loud: how leaders delegate, hold people accountable, get an outside view when the team is too close to the problem, and turn a setback into a turnaround. It is the exact register of Lesson 16 -- deciding who does what, and by when.

Tip: choose one episode on teams or accountability. Note two things the leader delegated, then record a 90-second recovery plan for a real AVRT problem using at least two causative structures.

Listen at ted.com →
YouTube
"How to use 'have something done'" -- BBC Learning English

A short, clear lesson on exactly the code from Lesson 16: the causative "have something done" and "get something done", used when you arrange for someone else to do the work. It shows the word order you practised -- have + object + past participle -- and how it differs from doing the job yourself.

Tip: pause after each example and say your own version about AVRT out loud. Then write four causative sentences about one real recovery -- at least one "have something done", one "get someone to do it", and one "have someone do it".

Watch on YouTube →
Welcome Key Words The Code Hotel Arts Practice Your Turn Wrap-Up
LESSON 1
T
